Three cases: (a) PURE BOARD/CEREMONY PLACEHOLDER — an empty or one-line body that merely names a process ceremony or board column (a backlog, a milestone/sprint-release tracker, a presentation slot) AND carries NO deliverable work-item type label (hasDeliverableTypeLabel is 0; deliverable types are story/bug/task/feature/requirement/artifact/epic): emit NOT_APPLICABLE and stay silent — a board placeholder is not authored engineering work. (b) EMPTY DELIVERABLE — the body is empty or merely echoes the title (emptyOrTitleEcho is 1) BUT the issue carries a BUILDABLE deliverable work-item type label (hasDeliverableTypeLabel is 1, where a buildable type is one of story / bug / task / feature / requirement / epic): the empty body IS the defect — a maintainer cannot tell what to build or how to verify done — so emit assessment=BAD/MAJOR (no checkable outcome). PURE DESIGN-ARTIFACT EXCLUSION (apply before this branch fires): a pure design-artifact label — 'type::Core Artifact', 'artifact type::Model', 'artifact type::*' board-mechanics, or a bare 'artifact'/'core artifact' label that names a design deliverable rather than a buildable work-item — is NOT a buildable deliverable type for this branch: a contentless card carrying ONLY such design-artifact labels (and no story/bug/task/feature/requirement/epic label) does not get EMPTY-DELIVERABLE/MAJOR — route it to NOT_APPLICABLE (a design-artifact placeholder is not buildable engineering work whose 'done' this practice defines), or cap it at MINOR if it nonetheless carries substantive prose. An out-of-date BUILDABLE deliverable with no content is worse, not exempt; but a design-artifact-only card is excluded here. (c) UMBRELLA/REQUIREMENT CARD with prose (looksUmbrella is 1): its 'checkable outcome' is decomposition into child stories that each carry their own acceptance criteria — deliver that as GUIDANCE. An umbrella that HAS substantive prose but lacks a child-mapped definition-of-done is MINOR (a decomposition nudge), NEVER MAJOR: do not treat 'bundles several capabilities with no inline checklist' as a MAJOR no-checkable-outcome failure — for an umbrella that bundling is expected and the fix is to split into children, which is itself surfaced elsewhere, so keep this to a single MINOR nudge and avoid double-alarming. Reserve MAJOR for an umbrella that is ALSO contentless (empty/title-echo body). CONSISTENCY RAIL: a checkable outcome presupposes there is an act-on-able problem/deliverable to define 'done' for; if the issue states no act-on-able problem or requested capability at all (the body is a bare placeholder phrase with no asserted defect and no requested capability), this practice has nothing whose 'done' it could define -> NOT_APPLICABLE, never an assessment=BAD for a missing acceptance-criteria block. EPIC/CORE-REQUIREMENT CALIBRATION: when the issue is an epic or core-requirement (a label naming epic / core requirement / theme, or a body that bundles many capabilities of one feature area), the checkable outcome it needs is a definition-of-done expressed as a checklist mapped to its child stories, NOT an inline Given-When-Then. If a sub-issue rollup (sub_issues_total > 0) or quoted child-issue links already decompose the epic into tracked children, the outcome is sufficiently checkable through that decomposition — emit a PRESENT, GOOD strength (or at most INFO), never an assessment=BAD demanding an inline acceptance-criteria block. Severity by decomposition: an epic with NO child decomposition (sub_issues_total is 0 or absent) AND no quotable done-criteria is MAJOR — a maintainer cannot tell when ANY of it is done, the mentor's highest-leverage issue lesson; an epic/requirement card that carries SUBSTANTIVE PROSE (looksUmbrella=1 and emptyOrTitleEcho=0) but lacks a child-mapped DoD is MINOR whether or not sub_issues_total>0 — the prose gives a maintainer enough to decompose, so the gap is a decomposition nudge, not an unstartable failure. MAJOR is reserved for an epic that is ALSO contentless (emptyOrTitleEcho=1): a contentless epic with neither prose nor child decomposition is the only MAJOR no-checkable-outcome epic case.

STEP 2 - CHECKABLE OUTCOME: a checkable outcome is satisfied by ANY of the following, each requiring a quotable span: an explicit acceptance-criteria or Given-When-Then block; for a story, a who/what/why that makes the outcome testable; for a bug, a quotable expected-behavior, which serves as the implicit acceptance criterion (the fix is verified when observed behavior matches the stated expected behavior). If none of these is quotable, 'done' is undefined.

Guidance is feed-forward: if no checkable outcome is quotable, show a short Given-When-Then or acceptance-criteria template, or — for a bug — point to stating the expected behavior that a fix must satisfy. Keep to this single gap.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: A checkable outcome is quotable — an acceptance-criteria or Given-When-Then block, or a story's who/what/why, or a bug's expected behavior that the fix must satisfy. A maintainer can tell when the issue is done, satisfying INVEST Testable.
- assessment=BAD: No checkable outcome is quotable, so 'done' is undefined: a story with no who/what/why, or a bug with no expected-behavior contrast, and no acceptance-criteria block. The feedback shows a Given-When-Then template or points to stating the expected behavior, so the next issue is verifiable.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue reads as a question, support request, or open discussion with no concrete deliverable, so a checkable outcome does not apply and no feedback is given. This abstention prevents demanding acceptance criteria of a discussion thread.
practices.whyItMatters: Without a clear line for 'done', nobody can tell when the work is finished, including the person doing it. A checkable outcome turns 'improve performance' from an open-ended wish into a task someone can actually complete.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A short acceptance checklist a maintainer can tick off when the work is truly finished, such as 'export downloads a valid CSV' and 'an empty result shows a friendly message', rather than a vague hope of improvement.

areas.name: Reviewing a teammate's work constructively
areas.description: Review a teammate's change well. Leave specific, actionable comments, raise concerns as questions rather than commands, and read the change before approving it rather than waving it through.

practices.name: Leave specific, actionable review comments
practices.criteria:
Assess, from the reviewer comment thread in comments.json[] (inline, diff-anchored) AND inputs/context/general_comments.json[] (the conversation-tab review notes, each {author, body, createdAt}, no path/line), whether the REVIEWER's substantive inline comments are useful — each one names a concrete construct or line and states what is wrong or what to change (ideally why) — rather than content-free gestures. Every entry in comments.json[] is an inline comment anchored to a diff line (it carries path, line, body, author, created_at); general_comments.json[] additionally carries the conversation-tab review notes — judge those for usefulness too, by the same standard (a useful comment names a concrete construct and states what to change, ideally why; a general comment quotes a path/symbol/term or asks a pointed question). Identify REVIEWER comments as those whose author login differs from metadata.json 'author' (the PR author) — you judge the reviewer's craft here, never the author's. You MAY read the hunk a comment anchors to (its path/line, and the corresponding [L<n>] span in diff.patch) to judge whether the comment actually points at something concrete, but assess the comment's USEFULNESS as a habit, not the underlying code's correctness, and never assert the reviewer's claim about the code is right or wrong.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ONLY the quality of the comments the reviewer WROTE. It does NOT judge whether the AUTHOR took the comments up or left threads dangling (that is engaging-with-inline-review-comments) — never reason about author replies, follow-up commits, or open/closed threads here. It does NOT judge the comment's TONE or politeness (that is reviews-respectfully-asks-rather-than-demands) — a blunt but specific comment is useful here; a polite but vague one is not. Judge specificity and actionability, nothing else.

## ENUMERATE EVERY REVIEW NOTE FIRST (source-agnostic — run before any field-name check)
Before deciding anything, build ONE candidate list of every review note present in your context regardless of which field or heading carries it: every entry of comments.json[], every entry of general_comments.json[], AND every note rendered under any 'discussion', 'discussions', 'threads', or 'review notes' heading in the artifact text. Treat a note as a candidate purely because it is a review note — never skip it because the literal field 'comments.json' or the 'enriched' flag is not present. Then run the procedure in order: (1) ENUMERATE all candidate notes; (2) DROP each note the exclusion filter below removes (author self-notes, Hephaestus/automation output, bare approvals, author-uptake/status replies, off-topic chatter); (3) COUNT the survivors as R; (4) CLASSIFY each survivor useful-vs-vague. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE ONLY when, after this enumeration and dropping, R == 0 — i.e. the thread is genuinely EMPTY of substantive reviewer notes. Never NA merely because a particular field name or flag is absent while substantive reviewer notes are visible.

## Exclusion filter (observable, apply before counting)
From comments.json[] AND general_comments.json[], keep only SUBSTANTIVE reviewer comments and SKIP a comment if any clause holds: (i) its author login equals metadata.json 'author' (an author self-note, not a review comment); (ii) its body contains the marker '<!-- hephaestus-diff-note -->' or '<!-- hephaestus' (Hephaestus's own posted notes); (iii) its author login is a recognized AUTOMATION account — its login — lowercased and with any trailing '[bot]' suffix stripped BEFORE matching — matches a known automation name-stem {coderabbitai, coderabbit, dependabot, renovate, github-actions, gitlab-bot, sonarcloud, codecov, sentry}, or ends in '[bot]', or contains 'hephaestus' (match the STEM even when the context projection has already stripped the '[bot]' suffix — a login stored as 'coderabbitai' with no suffix is STILL the CodeRabbit bot and MUST be excluded from human-reviewer credit) — but NOT a GitLab access-token identity of the form 'group_<id>_bot_<hash>' or 'project_<id>_bot_<hash>', which is a HUMAN reviewer authenticating with a group/project access token and counts as a genuine reviewer (do NOT drop it); (iv) it is a bare approval or acknowledgement ('LGTM', '+1', 'approved', 'nice', a lone thumbs-up) — these carry no review content and are neither counted as useful nor as vague. (v) it is an AUTHOR-UPTAKE or STATUS reply that reports what was done rather than asking for a change — its body is dominated by 'done', 'fixed', 'addressed', 'good catch — addressed', 'updated in the latest commit', 'will do', or a thanks/acknowledgement of a prior comment — this is the author taking the review up, NOT a reviewer's review comment (see Non-overlap), so SKIP it: never count it in R, U, or V; (vi) it raises a topic with NO connection to any construct, symbol, path, or line in THIS diff — it names no diff element AND diff.patch contains no path or symbol it could be about — AND is plainly meta-process chatter rather than feedback on this change, so SKIP it rather than classifying it VAGUE; but if it objects to or questions something that DOES appear in this diff (for example a config or deployment file the PR actually edits) it is on-topic — keep it and classify it normally. Let R = the number of substantive reviewer comments that survive this filter.

## Useful vs vague (language-agnostic, per surviving comment)
Classify each surviving comment as USEFUL when it BOTH (a) points at a concrete construct — it names a symbol/function/variable/type/path in its body OR is anchored to a specific path+line whose hunk it clearly references — AND (b) states an actionable substance: what is wrong, what to change, a concrete alternative, or a specific question a maintainer can answer (Bosu/Greiler/Bird, MSR 2015, on what makes a review comment useful — it points at concrete code and identifies a functional/maintenance concern that can be acted on). Classify as VAGUE when it is content-free: a lone gesture ('this is messy', 'fix this', 'not great', 'cleanup', 'hmm', 'really?', a bare '?'), a generic style scold with no named target, or praise/criticism that names nothing actionable. A short comment can still be USEFUL when its anchor line plus body make the target and the ask unambiguous (e.g. 'this leaks the connection — close it in a finally' on a specific line) — judge content, not length. Quote every comment you classify, with its path:line. A comment that is genuinely about a DIFFERENT subject than this code change, or that merely acknowledges or reports the status of a prior review note, is NOT a vague review comment — it was already removed by the exclusion filter and must never be swept into V here.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let U = the number of USEFUL surviving comments and V = the number of VAGUE surviving comments (U + V = R). Compute vagueFraction = V / R. State R, U, V, and quote the deciding comments (every vague one, and at least one useful one when U > 0).

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
V == 0 -> PRESENT, GOOD (every substantive comment is specific and actionable). V >= 1 with vagueFraction <= 0.25 -> INFO (a few stragglers; the gentlest nudge). 0.25 < vagueFraction <= 0.50 -> MINOR (several comments leave the author guessing). vagueFraction > 0.50 -> MAJOR (the review is predominantly content-free, so the author cannot tell what to change). Single-comment exception: when R == 1 and that one comment is vague, cap at MINOR — one unhelpful note is a light cost. Identical counts must always yield identical severity. Never exceed MAJOR on comment quality alone. (The 0.25 and 0.50 vagueFraction cutoffs are catalogue-chosen operational thresholds for consistency, not values reported in the cited literature.)

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Emit NOT_APPLICABLE only when BOTH comment sources are empty of substantive reviewer comments — i.e. comments.json[] AND general_comments.json[] (and any review-discussion thread materialized in the artifact text) are all absent or empty. A NON-EMPTY discussion thread IS the materialization signal: do NOT abstain on a missing metadata.enriched flag or a missing field name when a reviewer comment is plainly present in your context; read it and judge it. Use the precompute comment arrays when present; otherwise read the review notes directly from any discussion/thread section of the artifact text. After the exclusion filter, if R == 0 (the common case for a newly-opened PR: comments.json[] is empty, or holds only the author's own notes, Hephaestus/bot output, or bare approvals), emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no reviewer comment whose usefulness could be assessed; never fabricate a vague comment to avoid looking idle. Every PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding MUST state R, U, V and quote the deciding comments verbatim with their path:line. A 'vague' classification REQUIRES the quoted body; never call a comment vague without quoting it. EVIDENCE-LOCATION HYGIENE (mandatory): only an INLINE comment carries a path:line — emit an evidence.location only when it is literally that comment's own anchored position; a conversation-tab / general comment has no position, so its evidence.locations MUST be empty (quote it by author and body, never invent a file:line). comments.json[] is judged comment-by-comment — never reason about thread ordering, author uptake, or whether the reviewer's concern was correct.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when R >= 1 (at least one substantive reviewer comment survives the filter) and enriched=true. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. never grade the reviewer as a person — talk about the comments. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential vague comment and show the shape of a useful version (name the construct + state the concrete ask), never rewrite the whole review.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: At least one substantive reviewer comment survives the filter (R >= 1, stated) and the clear majority are useful — each quoted useful comment points at a concrete construct or anchored line and states an actionable concern or question (V == 0, or vagueFraction <= 0.25 as a light INFO), so the author can tell exactly what to change.
- assessment=BAD: Substantive reviewer comments are predominantly content-free (vagueFraction > 0.25), each vague comment quoted with its path:line — graded by severity: INFO when vagueFraction <= 0.25, MINOR when 0.25 < vagueFraction <= 0.50 (or a lone vague single comment), MAJOR when vagueFraction > 0.50. Naming the construct and stating the concrete change for the noisiest comment would let the author act without a clarification round-trip.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false, or comments.json[] is absent, or after excluding the author's own notes, Hephaestus/bot output, and bare approvals there is no substantive reviewer comment (R == 0), so review-comment usefulness cannot be assessed.
practices.whyItMatters: 'This looks off' leaves the author guessing what to change and why. A comment that points at the exact line and names the concern gets fixed, while a vague one just slows the change down.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A note anchored to the line that says 'this throws when items is empty, so guard the loop or return early on a null list', so the author knows exactly what to do next.

practices.name: Comment on the code, not the person
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the REVIEWER's inline comments stay civil and collaborative — concerns raised as questions or suggestions and disagreement reasoned ('I would prefer X because Y') rather than bare commands, belittling, sarcasm, name-calling, or contempt. Framed as a communication habit about how feedback is voiced, never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR on tone alone. Read comments.json[] (each inline reviewer comment carries path, line, body, author, created_at) AND inputs/context/general_comments.json[] (each conversation-tab reviewer comment carries author, body, createdAt — no path/line). Judge tone across BOTH sources, applying the same author!=metadata.author + bot/hephaestus + bare-approval exclusion filter to each. Do NOT read the diff to re-judge whether the underlying technical point is right — tone is the only surface here.

## What this does NOT judge (non-overlap — enforce strictly)
This practice does NOT judge whether a comment is specific, actionable, or technically useful — that is assessed elsewhere and is out of scope here. A blunt-but-civil 'This will NPE when items is empty' is on-topic and respectful: do NOT flag it here merely for being terse, direct, or critical. Only the TONE markers below count. A comment can be vague AND civil (a PRESENT, GOOD strength here, the sibling handles vagueness) or specific AND uncivil (assessment=BAD here). Never escalate severity because the point is also unactionable.

## Who is a reviewer (mandatory filter)
A reviewer comment is one whose author login DIFFERS from metadata.json author (the PR author) — never grade the author's own comments. Apply this observable exclusion filter and SKIP a comment if any clause holds: (i) its body contains the marker '<!-- hephaestus-diff-note -->' or '<!-- hephaestus' (Hephaestus's own notes), (ii) its author login is a recognized AUTOMATION account — its login — lowercased and with any trailing '[bot]' suffix stripped BEFORE matching — matches a known automation name-stem {coderabbitai, coderabbit, dependabot, renovate, github-actions, gitlab-bot, sonarcloud, codecov, sentry}, or ends in '[bot]', or contains 'hephaestus' (match the STEM even when the context projection has already stripped the '[bot]' suffix — a login stored as 'coderabbitai' with no suffix is STILL the CodeRabbit bot and MUST be excluded from human-reviewer credit) — but NOT a GitLab access-token identity of the form 'group_<id>_bot_<hash>' or 'project_<id>_bot_<hash>', which is a HUMAN reviewer authenticating with a group/project access token and counts as a genuine reviewer (do NOT drop it), or (iii) it is a bare approval/acknowledgement ('LGTM', '+1', 'approved', a lone thumbs-up). Let R = the count of remaining substantive reviewer comments.

## Incivility markers (language-agnostic; quote the exact span)
Count a reviewer comment as UNCIVIL only when you can quote a span carrying a clear marker, not on a hunch: (a) a bare imperative DEMAND with no rationale and no collaborative softening ('Change this.', 'Remove it.', 'Do it the other way.' standing alone) — a command paired with a reason ('Use a Set here because the lookup is O(n)') is NOT a demand; (b) BELITTLING — 'just', 'obviously', 'simply', 'clearly', 'why did you not / why didn't you', 'everyone knows', 'as I already said', used to imply the author should not have needed telling; (c) SARCASM or rhetorical contempt ('great job breaking the build', 'did you even test this', 'wow'); (d) NAME-CALLING or judging the person rather than the code ('this is lazy', 'sloppy', 'you clearly don't understand', 'amateur', 'nonsense', 'garbage'); (e) profanity or hostility directed at the author or their work. A lone 'just' in a genuinely neutral sentence ('just a nit:', 'just below this line') is NOT belittling — judge it in context and quote it. Never psychoanalyze the reviewer or infer intent beyond the quoted words; never treat directness, brevity, or a strong technical disagreement as incivility on its own.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let U = the number of substantive reviewer comments carrying at least one quotable incivility marker, of R total. State R and U and quote every offending span you counted (comment author, the marker text, and which marker type a–e it is).

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
U == 0 -> PRESENT, GOOD (every reviewer comment is civil and collaborative). U >= 1 with at most a single comment carrying only a mild marker — exactly one comment, and its only markers are type (a) bare demand or (b) belittling — -> INFO (a single sharp edge; the gentlest nudge toward asking rather than telling). 2 to 3 uncivil comments, OR a single comment carrying a type (c)/(d)/(e) marker (sarcasm, name-calling, hostility) -> MINOR. More than 3 uncivil comments, OR any comment combining name-calling/hostility (d/e) with a personal target -> MAJOR (the review tone has turned adversarial and is likely to chill collaboration). Tone never exceeds MAJOR. Identical comments must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every present marker MUST be quoted verbatim with its author login; a marker you cannot quote is counted absent. A PRESENT, GOOD strength on a non-empty thread states R and confirms U == 0 after reading each reviewer comment. An assessment=BAD finding quotes each offending span. EVIDENCE-LOCATION HYGIENE (mandatory): never emit an evidence.location whose path/line is not literally the position of the comment you are citing — for an inline comment use ONLY the path+line that comment actually carries; for a conversation-tab / general comment (no path/line in its position) evidence.locations MUST be empty (cite the comment by its author and quoted text, never a fabricated file:line). Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior. Frame all wording about the COMMENT ('this phrasing reads as a demand'), never about the reviewer as a person ('you are rude').

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when R >= 1 (at least one substantive reviewer comment survives the exclusion filter). Guidance is feed-forward and shows the SHAPE of the civil rephrase, not a rewrite of the whole comment: turn the single most consequential demand into a question or add the missing 'because' ('Could we use a Set here? — the lookup is O(n) otherwise'), naming only that one comment.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: At least one substantive reviewer comment is quotable and every one is civil and collaborative — concerns posed as questions/suggestions or disagreement carrying a reason (U == 0 of R, with R stated). The feedback stays on the code and invites a response.
- assessment=BAD: At least one substantive reviewer comment carries a quotable incivility marker (U >= 1 of R, each span quoted with its type a–e) — graded by severity: INFO for a single mild demand/belittling edge, MINOR for 2–3 uncivil comments or a single sarcasm/name-calling span, MAJOR for more than 3 or a personally-targeted hostile/name-calling comment. Rephrasing the named comment as a question or attaching the reason behind the ask would keep the exchange collaborative.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: After the exclusion filter, comments.json[] holds no substantive reviewer comments (the common case for a newly-opened PR) — it is empty, or carries only the PR author's own comments, Hephaestus/bot output, or bare approvals — so there is no reviewer tone to assess; never fabricate one.
practices.whyItMatters: The same point can be made as a question or as a put-down, and people remember which one you chose. A curious question gets acted on, while sarcasm and bare orders breed defensiveness and make the next review harder.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: 'What happens if this runs twice, could we hit a race here?' rather than 'this is obviously broken, did you even test it?', so disagreement lands on the code and never on the author.

practices.name: Read the change before approving it
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the REVIEWER engaged with this change in proportion to its size and risk, rather than rubber-stamping it — framed as a reviewing habit about whether the review shows the diff was read, never as a judgement of the reviewer as a person and never exceeding MAJOR. A review engages when at least one reviewer comment is anchored to a concrete part of the diff (it names a file/path, a symbol, a line, or quotes/paraphrases a term that actually appears in diff.patch or diff_summary.md) OR asks a genuine clarifying question about the change; it rubber-stamps when a non-trivial change drew nothing but a bare approval.

## Decision-level rubber-stamp signal (inputs/context/review_threads.json)
The substance of a review is not always an inline comment — an APPROVED decision with no anchored comment IS the rubber-stamp. When review_threads.json is present, read reviewDecisions[] (each {state, author}): a decision with state 'APPROVED' by a reviewer whose login differs from metadata.author confirms a review HAPPENED. If that approval exists on a SIZEABLE/RISKY change AND, after the exclusion filter, NO substantive reviewer comment remains in EITHER comments.json[] (inline) OR inputs/context/general_comments.json[] (the conversation-tab discussion, see below) — and no UNRESOLVED thread carries a substantive note — that is a rubber-stamp: the change was approved without an observable engaged reading. Emit assessment=BAD on that basis only when BOTH comment sources are empty of substantive reviewer comments after the exclusion filter; the emptiness of BOTH is the signal the APPROVED decision was unengaged. CRITICAL: if EITHER source carries one substantive reviewer comment, the review WAS engaged and this is NOT a rubber-stamp — never fire this assessment=BAD finding then. Most student review happens in the conversation tab (general_comments.json), not on diff lines, so an empty inline comments.json alone NEVER proves a rubber-stamp. Quote the approving reviewer's login. When review_threads.json is absent or shows no APPROVED reviewer decision, fall back to the comments-only rule and abstain on an empty comment thread (a not-yet-reviewed PR is NOT a rubber-stamp).

## Reviewer comments (who counts)
ENUMERATE FIRST, SOURCE-AGNOSTICALLY: a review note is a candidate regardless of which field or heading carries it — comments.json[], general_comments.json[], OR any note under a 'discussion'/'discussions'/'threads'/'review notes' heading in the artifact text are ALL reviewer-signal sources. Never decide a review was un-engaged because a particular field name ('comments.json'/'enriched') is not literally present while substantive reviewer notes are visible; read the visible notes and apply the who-counts/exclusion rules to them. Read comments.json[] (inline, diff-anchored) AND inputs/context/general_comments.json[] (the conversation-tab / non-anchored review notes GitLab stores separately, each {author, body, createdAt} with no path/line) for reviewer signal — a review counts as engaged if a substantive reviewer comment survives the filter in EITHER source. When those named precompute files are absent but a review-discussion / notes / threads section IS materialized in the artifact text (a list of {author, body} review notes), treat THAT section as the comment source and apply the same who-counts and exclusion rules to it — never abstain on a missing field name while a reviewer comment is plainly visible. Every entry is an inline comment anchored to a diff line (it carries path, line, body, author). A REVIEWER comment is one whose author login differs from metadata.author (the PR author). Apply this observable exclusion filter and DROP a comment if any clause holds: (i) its body contains '<!-- hephaestus-diff-note -->' or '<!-- hephaestus' (Hephaestus's own posted notes); (ii) its author login is a recognized AUTOMATION account — its login — lowercased and with any trailing '[bot]' suffix stripped BEFORE matching — matches a known automation name-stem {coderabbitai, coderabbit, dependabot, renovate, github-actions, gitlab-bot, sonarcloud, codecov, sentry}, or ends in '[bot]', or contains 'hephaestus' (match the STEM even when the context projection has already stripped the '[bot]' suffix — a login stored as 'coderabbitai' with no suffix is STILL the CodeRabbit bot and MUST be excluded from human-reviewer credit) — but NOT a GitLab access-token identity of the form 'group_<id>_bot_<hash>' or 'project_<id>_bot_<hash>', which is a HUMAN reviewer authenticating with a group/project access token and counts as a genuine reviewer (do NOT drop it); (iii) it is a bare approval or acknowledgement (body is, after trimming, only 'LGTM', '+1', 'approved', 'ship it', a lone thumbs-up/emoji, or equivalent with no diff anchor and no question). Apply the SAME exclusion filter (author!=metadata.author; drop hephaestus/bot logins and the '<!-- hephaestus' marker; drop bare approvals) to general_comments.json[] entries; a substantive GENERAL comment EITHER names/quotes a concrete diff element OR poses a clarifying question about the change. What remains after the filter, across BOTH sources, is the set of SUBSTANTIVE reviewer comments. A substantive comment is one that EITHER names/quotes a concrete diff element (path appearing in diff_summary.md, or a symbol/term that appears in a diff.patch hunk — quote the matching term from BOTH the comment and the hunk) OR poses a clarifying question (ends in '?' or requests information about the change) — quote it.

## Source of size (single authoritative source)
Gauge change size from the integer fields metadata.additions, metadata.deletions, metadata.changed_files WHEN PRESENT; compute net changed lines as additions + deletions, and state the exact figures you used. When those precompute integers are ABSENT, estimate size from any diff materialized in your context — count the changed-line and changed-file evidence visible in diff.patch / diff_summary.md / the diff section of the artifact text — and state that the figures are an observed estimate. Only when NEITHER the integers NOR any diff is observable is size genuinely un-gaugeable. These are raw DB totals with no generated/vendored exclusion, so sanity-check against the diff_summary.md file-path table: if generated/vendored paths (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, OpenAPI specs, route/router trees, dist/, snapshots, DB migrations) account for at least 70% of the changed files OR of the +Lines, treat the change as effectively smaller for the trivial-size test below.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
This practice only ever fires assessment=BAD at MINOR or MAJOR; it never assigns INFO. A change is TRIVIAL when, after the generated/vendored sanity-check, additions + deletions <= 20 AND changed_files <= 2. A change is SIZEABLE/RISKY when additions + deletions >= 50 OR changed_files >= 5. Between those bands (a small-but-not-trivial change) treat as SIZEABLE only if changed_files >= 3. assessment=BAD severity: MAJOR when the change is SIZEABLE/RISKY (additions + deletions >= 200 OR changed_files >= 10) yet every reviewer comment was filtered out as a bare approval/acknowledgement, so a substantial diff received no engaged reading; MINOR when the change is SIZEABLE/RISKY below that extreme (50-199 net lines, or 3-9 changed files) and likewise drew only bare approvals. Identical inputs (same counts, same comment set) must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Emit NOT_APPLICABLE on size grounds only when change size is unobservable from EVERY source — the precompute integers metadata.additions / metadata.deletions / metadata.changed_files are absent AND no diff (diff.patch / diff_summary.md / a diff section of the artifact text) is materialized either, OR the observable change is genuinely empty (changed_files == 0 AND additions + deletions == 0). Do NOT abstain merely because the metadata.enriched flag or the integer fields are missing while a diff and a review thread are plainly visible in your context: read the visible thread against the size you can observe. Never read missing counts as zero. Every finding MUST state the exact additions, deletions, total, and changed_files it used. Every PRESENT, GOOD strength MUST quote the substantive reviewer comment AND, when the basis is diff-anchoring, the matching term from the diff.patch/diff_summary.md it anchors to. Every assessment=BAD finding MUST state the size counts AND confirm that, after the exclusion filter, zero substantive reviewer comments remain (i.e. every reviewer comment was a bare approval/acknowledgement).

## Non-overlap (enforce — do not double-flag a sibling)
Judge ONLY whether the review engaged proportional to change size. Do NOT grade the SPECIFICITY, wording, tone, or correctness of reviewer comments, and do NOT grade whether the AUTHOR took the comments up — those are assessed by other checks and are out of scope here (author follow-up, and comment specificity/tone, are each judged elsewhere). The presence of any one quotable substantive reviewer comment is sufficient for a PRESENT, GOOD strength here regardless of how polished it is.

## Observation & abstention
Reason only from observable comments versus change size — never claim to know what the reviewer understood internally; the diff anchor or the question IS the observable engagement signal. Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when the size integers are present (enriched=true). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward and addressed to the review habit, not the reviewer: name the single most consequential gap and point forward to the one next step (e.g. anchor a comment to the specific hunk that carries the most risk, or ask the one clarifying question the diff raises), never rewrite the whole review.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: The change is non-trivial and, after the exclusion filter, at least one substantive reviewer comment remains that either anchors to a concrete part of the diff (quote the comment AND the matching path/symbol/term it shares with diff.patch or diff_summary.md) or asks a genuine clarifying question (quote it) — evidence the diff was actually read rather than waved through. State the exact size counts.
- assessment=BAD: The change is SIZEABLE/RISKY (state the exact additions, deletions, total, and changed_files) yet after the exclusion filter zero substantive reviewer comments remain in EITHER comments.json[] OR general_comments.json[] — every reviewer signal across both sources was a bare approval/acknowledgement ('LGTM', '+1', 'approved', a lone thumbs-up), so a substantial diff drew no engaged reading. State explicitly that BOTH sources were checked and both empty. Severity is MAJOR when additions + deletions >= 200 OR changed_files >= 10, otherwise MINOR. Point forward to anchoring one comment to the riskiest hunk or asking the one clarifying question the change raises.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or the size integers are absent, so change size cannot be gauged; OR the change is TRIVIAL (after the generated/vendored sanity-check, <= 20 net lines AND <= 2 changed files), where a lone approval is a reasonable review and demands no commentary; OR no reviewer acted at all AND review_threads.json shows no APPROVED reviewer decision — after the exclusion filter comments.json[] holds no reviewer comments (only the author, Hephaestus/bot output, or an empty list) and no approving review decision exists, so there is no review whose substance could be assessed (the common case for a newly-opened PR). When review_threads.json DOES carry an APPROVED reviewer decision on a sizeable change with no substantive comment, that is the rubber-stamp assessment=BAD above, not NOT_APPLICABLE. Never fabricate a reviewer finding to avoid looking idle.
practices.whyItMatters: A quick 'looks good' on a risky change gives everyone false confidence, and the bug lands with an approval attached to it. A review only protects the codebase when someone actually read the diff.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: An approval that comes with at least one comment showing the diff was read, such as a question about an edge case or a note on a tricky function, sized to how much the change touches.

areas.name: Testing your changes
areas.description: When a change adds or fixes behaviour, it comes with the tests that exercise it. And it never quietly deletes, skips, or weakens a test just to make the build pass.

practices.name: Include tests with the change
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the non-trivial production logic introduced or changed in this PR arrives together with test code that exercises THAT logic, framed as a workflow habit about how the change is delivered — never as a hunt for missing edge cases, a lint sweep, or a re-judging of the change's size (that is scope-one-reviewable-change). Read diff.patch and classify each changed file by its path before judging anything else; quote the exact [L<n>] span you rely on. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice. ESPECIALLY: a change that fixes a defect should ship a test that asserts the now-correct behaviour.

## Source of files (single authoritative classification)
Partition the hand-written files in diff.patch into TEST files and PRODUCTION files by path/name, language-agnostically. A file is a TEST file when its path or name matches a recognised test convention: a segment named test/tests/spec/specs/__tests__/testing, a basename suffix such as *_test.go, *_test.py, *.test.ts/js/tsx/jsx, *.spec.ts/js, *Test.java/*Tests.java/*IT.java, test_*.py, *.test.* generally, or a fixtures/snapshots directory beside such files. Everything else hand-written is a PRODUCTION file. FIRST exclude generated/vendored paths from BOTH sets — dependency lock files (*.lock, *-lock.*, go.sum), code-generation output (*.gen.*, *.pb.*, OpenAPI specs, router/route trees), committed build artifacts (dist/), snapshots that are not hand-authored assertions, and DB migrations — they are neither production logic nor a test of it. Assess only the hand-written remainder. State the production files and the test files you counted.

## What counts as non-trivial production logic (the surface this practice applies to)
Production logic worth a test is added/changed behaviour a test could observe: a new or modified function/method/endpoint/handler, a new conditional or loop branch, a changed computation, validation, parsing, error handling, or state transition. It is NOT: pure renames, signature-only churn, import reordering, comment/whitespace/formatting, type-only declarations, constant/config value tweaks, string/copy edits, dependency bumps, or wiring that merely re-exports or registers existing behaviour. Quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that embody the logic you judge non-trivial; if you cannot quote an added behavioural line, treat the change as carrying no testable production surface.

## What counts as a covering test
An added or modified test file in the SAME diff is covering when it plausibly exercises the changed production logic — it references the same symbol/function/endpoint/path, asserts on the new branch's behaviour, or (for a defect fix) asserts the corrected result. You judge PRESENCE and PLAUSIBLE LINKAGE of a test for the changed logic, NOT the test's readability, naming, or assertion style (those are out of scope). Quote the added [L<n>] test span and name the production symbol it touches. A test file that is only reformatted, or that exercises unrelated code, does not count — say so.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Let P = whether the diff adds/changes non-trivial production logic (quotable), and C = whether the same diff adds/changes a covering test for it (quotable). DEFECT-FIX flag: set when the title/body or a commit subject signals a bug fix (fix/fixes/bug/regression/hotfix/broken/incorrect + a concrete object) AND the production change alters existing behaviour rather than adding greenfield code. Buckets: P true AND C false AND defect-fix -> MAJOR (a corrected behaviour ships with nothing locking it in, so the regression can silently return). P true AND C false AND (repoTestFileCount==0 OR repoTestFileCount is unavailable/absent this run) -> severity by a COUNTABLE size of the untested production surface (no discretion): MINOR when the whole quotable production surface is a single added/changed non-trivial symbol whose added behavioural span is <=10 lines and contains no nested conditional, loop, or error/exception path (a focused setter/reset/getter or flat one-symbol change ships into a repo with no test target — still a lesson, but not the suite's top severity); MAJOR when the diff adds 2+ distinct non-trivial production symbols, OR any single symbol whose added behavioural span exceeds 10 lines, OR any added symbol containing a nested conditional/loop/error path (load-bearing logic ships into a repo with NO test target at all — there is no safety net to lock the behaviour in; this is the escalation, applied HERE, not deferred). Count added behavioural lines by the same [L<n>] spans you already quoted, excluding blanks, comments, signature-only and import lines; identical input must always yield identical severity. This bucket is the authoritative fixed-severity determinant whenever repoTestFileCount is 0 OR unavailable this run, and overrides the 'MINOR otherwise' phrasing in the assessment=BAD observation block. P true AND C false AND defect-fix (repo HAS a test target) -> MAJOR. P true AND C false AND NOT defect-fix (repo HAS a test target) -> MINOR (a single focused test would pin it).

## Repository has no test target — ESCALATE, do NOT abstain
The ships-tests precompute emits repoTestFileCount (and diffProductionFiles / diffTestFiles) — re-verifiable by searching inputs/sources/scm/repo for *Test*/*Tests*/*Spec* / test|Tests dirs / XCTest|@Test. When the change adds testable production logic AND the repository contains NO test files anywhere (repoTestFileCount == 0), DO NOT emit NOT_APPLICABLE: the absence of any test target on load-bearing, testable logic is the MOST consequential testing lesson, not an excuse to abstain. Emit assessment=BAD and grade severity by the repoTestFileCount==0 bucket above (MINOR for a single <=10-line flat symbol with no nested branch; MAJOR for 2+ non-trivial symbols, any >10-line symbol, or any nested conditional/loop/error path) — the change ships untested logic into a project with no test culture, so both a covering test and standing up a test target are warranted, but the severity follows the countable split, not a blanket MAJOR. This is DISTINCT from keeps-the-test-suite-honest (which owns weakening an EXISTING suite), so the two may co-exist. When test files DO exist (repoTestFileCount > 0) assess the MINOR/MAJOR buckets normally — untested logic added to a repo that DOES test is a MINOR/MAJOR finding. P true AND C true -> PRESENT, GOOD. Never escalate above MAJOR. Identical input must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If diff.patch is absent or empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. If — after excluding generated/vendored paths — the change is test-only (every hand-written production line is itself in a test file), or docs-only, or config-only, or a pure refactor/rename/formatting/type-only change with NO quotable added behavioural line, emit NOT_APPLICABLE: there is no production surface this practice applies to, and a change that is itself all tests already satisfies the habit — never invent untested logic to manufacture an assessment=BAD finding. An assessment=BAD finding REQUIRES a quoted added [L<n>] production line embodying the untested behaviour AND a stated, completed scan of every test file in the diff confirming none covers it. A PRESENT, GOOD strength REQUIRES both the quoted production [L<n>] span and the quoted covering-test [L<n>] span naming the shared symbol. If the diff is dominated (>=90% of hand-written +lines) by generated/vendored files so no hand-written production surface remains, emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Observation & abstention
Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential untested behaviour and show the SHAPE of the test to add (the symbol to call and the one assertion that would pin the new branch or the fixed bug), never a finished test body and never a rewrite of their change.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: The diff adds or changes non-trivial production logic (quoted [L<n>]) AND the same diff adds or updates a test that exercises it (quoted [L<n>], naming the shared symbol/endpoint), so the new behaviour ships locked in by a test; or the change is test-only and already embodies the habit.
- assessment=BAD: The diff adds or changes a quotable piece of non-trivial production logic — a new function/endpoint/branch or a defect fix (quote the added [L<n>] line) — yet no test file in the diff exercises it (state the test files scanned, or that none exist). Graded by the Severity block above (apply it exactly, do not shortcut): MAJOR for a defect fix shipping with no test, OR when the untested surface is 2+ distinct non-trivial symbols / a single symbol whose added span exceeds 10 lines / any added nested conditional, loop, or error path; MINOR only when the whole untested surface is a single small flat symbol (<=10 lines, no nested branch). A single focused test calling that symbol and asserting the new branch's result would ship the behaviour with a guard around it.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: diff.patch is absent/empty; or the change is test-only, docs-only, config-only, or a pure refactor/rename/formatting/type-only diff with no quotable added behavioural production line; or generated/vendored paths dominate (>=90% of hand-written +lines), so there is no production surface whose test coverage could be assessed. Inability to enumerate the repository's existing tests is NEVER a basis for NOT_APPLICABLE: the observation is decided from the diff ALONE — non-trivial production logic added with no covering test in the same diff is assessment=BAD even when repoTestFileCount is absent; repoTestFileCount only refines severity, never the observation.
practices.whyItMatters: Code without a test is a promise nobody can check, and it is one careless refactor away from quietly breaking. Adding the test alongside the logic locks in the behaviour you just built, which matters most when you are fixing a bug.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: New logic arrives with a test beside it, and a bug fix comes with a test that fails on the old code and passes on the new, proving the behaviour is what you say it is.

practices.name: Keep the test suite honest
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this change keeps the existing test suite honest — i.e. whether it deletes, disables/skips, or hollows out a test or its assertions to get a build green WITHOUT a stated, quotable reason — framed as a workflow/craft habit about how the change treats its safety net, never as a line-by-line lint sweep and never exceeding MAJOR on test-suppression alone. Read the actual change: scan diff.patch for hand-written test-file hunks and judge ONLY the added/removed lines and their immediate in-diff context. NEVER claim to run/compile/execute the suite, and never assert a suppression you cannot point to in a quoted [L<n>] span (for a removal, quote the removed line from the hunk).

## Non-overlap (enforce — do not double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ONLY whether the change WEAKENS the existing safety net. It does NOT judge whether NEW tests were added to cover the new logic — that absence is assessed by a different check (INTERNAL ROUTING — do not write this relationship into reasoning or guidance). A change that adds no test is NOT a finding here; a feature with zero test changes is NOT_APPLICABLE here. Only an observable removal/skip/weakening of a test or assertion is in scope.

## What is a test surface
A test surface is a hand-written test file or test hunk: paths under test/, tests/, __tests__/, spec/, or files matching *Test.*, *Tests.*, *.test.*, *.spec.*, *_test.*, test_*.*, *Spec.*, conftest.py, or a hunk that adds/removes test-framework calls (assert*, expect(...), @Test, it(/test(/describe(, def test_). If the diff touches no such surface, this practice does not apply.

## What counts as suppression (language-agnostic — quote the exact line)
Count a suppression when a test hunk does ANY of the following and the change is NOT itself the legitimate deletion of dead/obsolete code (see legitimacy gate):
- Deletes a whole test method/case/`it`/`test`/`def test_`/`@Test` block (removed lines in a test file that drop an entire test).
- Disables/skips a test: adds @Disabled, @Ignore, @org.junit.jupiter.api.Disabled, xit(/xdescribe(/it.skip(/describe.skip(/test.skip(/fit-to-xit.skip(, @pytest.mark.skip / @pytest.mark.skipif / @unittest.skip, t.Skip( / t.SkipNow( (Go), #[ignore] (Rust), `return` early at the top of a test body, or wraps a test body in `if (false)` / `if 0:`.
- Comments out a test or an assertion (a removed/added line that turns executable test code into a comment).
- Weakens an assertion to trivially-true: replaces a real assertion with assertTrue(true), expect(true).toBe(true), assert True, assert 1 == 1, XCTAssertTrue(true), an empty test body where a body was removed, or loosens a concrete expected value to a tautology (e.g. assertEquals(expected, actual) -> assertNotNull(actual), or expect(x).toBe(42) -> expect(x).toBeDefined()) such that the check can no longer fail on the regression it guarded.
A mechanical migration that PRESERVES assertion strength is NOT suppression: renaming a test, moving it between files (removal in one file balanced by an equivalent addition quotable in another hunk), upgrading an assertion API (assertEquals -> assertThat(...).isEqualTo(...)), or splitting one test into several. When a removal is balanced by an equivalent or stronger added test you can quote, do not count it.

## Legitimacy gate (the quotable justification)
A suppression is JUSTIFIED — and therefore a PRESENT, GOOD strength rather than a finding — only when a reason is quotable from an OBSERVABLE surface tied to the suppression: an inline comment on or adjacent to the suppressing line in the same hunk (e.g. `// skip: flaky on CI, tracked in #482`, a `@Disabled(\"reason …\")` / `@pytest.mark.skip(reason=\"…\")` argument), OR the metadata.body / a metadata.commits[] subject that names the same test or file and states why (e.g. \"remove obsolete FooServiceTest — Foo deleted in this PR\"). You MUST quote BOTH the suppression line and the justification span. A bare skip annotation with no reason argument, an empty TODO, or 'temporarily disabled' with no tracker/why is NOT a justification. Deleting a test whose subject-under-test is itself deleted IN THIS SAME DIFF is justified when you can quote the corresponding production-code removal — name the file.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let S = the number of distinct suppressions you can quote (each with its [L<n>]/removed line), and J = the number of those that carry a quotable justification per the legitimacy gate. The UNJUSTIFIED count U = S - J. State S, J, and U, and quote every line you counted.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
U == 0 -> PRESENT, GOOD (no test or assertion was silently suppressed; any suppression present is justified and quoted). U == 1 with the suppression being a single disabled/skipped test (not a deleted assertion guarding behavior changed in this same PR) -> MINOR (one unexplained skip — name it and ask for the one-line reason or its removal). U >= 1 where any unjustified suppression DELETES a test or WEAKENS an assertion to trivially-true, OR U >= 2 of any kind -> MAJOR (the safety net is being lowered without a stated reason). Never exceed MAJOR on test-suppression alone. Identical diffs must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the deciding test line(s) from diff.patch (an [L<n>] span for an added skip/weakened assertion, or the removed line for a deletion) — never describe a suppression you cannot quote. A 'no suppression' PRESENT, GOOD strength requires that you actually inspected the test hunks (name the test file(s) you read); do NOT emit a PRESENT, GOOD strength for a diff that touches no test surface. If diff.patch is absent/empty, or the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, dist/, snapshots, migrations) leaving no hand-written test hunk, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. A justification claim REQUIRES the quoted reason span; absent it, the suppression counts as unjustified.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when the diff contains at least one hand-written test surface AND the deciding lines are quotable. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE — most commonly a diff that touches no tests at all, or a docs/config-only change. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential suppressed test, show the shape of the fix (restore the assertion, or add a one-line `reason`/tracker to the skip), and never rewrite their whole test file or grade the author.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: The change touches at least one hand-written test surface (name it) and removes/disables/weakens no test or assertion (U == 0), OR every suppression present carries a quotable justification (the skip's reason argument, an adjacent comment, or a commit/body span naming the same test) — both the suppression line and its reason quoted — so the safety net is preserved or its narrowing is explained.
- assessment=BAD: At least one unjustified suppression is quotable (U >= 1): a deleted test block, an added @Disabled/.skip/xit/@pytest.mark.skip/t.Skip with no reason, a commented-out assertion, or an assertion weakened to trivially-true — graded by severity (MINOR for a single unexplained skip; MAJOR when a test is deleted, an assertion is hollowed, or two-plus suppressions land unexplained). Quote the suppression line(s), state S/J/U, and point forward to restoring the check or adding the one-line reason that says why it is gone.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: diff.patch is absent/empty, the change touches no hand-written test surface (a docs-only, config-only, or production-only change with no test hunk), or generated/vendored files dominate so no hand-written test hunk remains — so there is no existing test the change could be lowering, and the absence of NEW tests is assessed by a different check, not this one.
practices.whyItMatters: Deleting or skipping a failing test to get a passing build hides the breakage instead of fixing it, and removes the alarm that would have caught it next time. The suite only protects you while it tells the truth.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: When a test starts failing, the change fixes the underlying cause, and if the test genuinely needs to go, it is removed with a one-line note on why, never quietly skipped to get a passing build.

areas.name: Writing maintainable code
areas.description: Keep the changed code easy for the next person to read and change. Factor out duplication, keep units small and single-purpose, and leave no clutter behind, such as dead code, debug leftovers, or comments that just restate the line.

practices.name: Factor out duplication instead of copy-pasting
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect — copy-pasted near-identical logic. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds logic but no two-or-more near-identical blocks are quotable. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health asserting 'no duplication was introduced', which you CANNOT verify: the block this change may be copy-pasting from often lives OUTSIDE the added lines (a pre-existing pipeline, a sibling file not in the diff), so 'I saw no duplicate' is an unverifiable negative claim, not an observed good act. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable pair of near-identical added blocks) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable duplicated pair, or no surface). Assess whether this change factors out repetition rather than copy-pasting near-identical non-trivial logic, framed as a craft habit about how the added code is structured — never as a code-defect, correctness, or runtime claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY the lines this PR ADDS (lines marked with an [L<n>] new-side marker in an added hunk); pre-existing duplication you cannot see being added in this diff is out of scope. This practice judges REPETITION across two-or-more added blocks; it does NOT judge how long any single function is or whether one function does one thing (that is keeps-functions-small-and-single-purpose) — never flag a single long block here, only two-or-more near-identical ones.

## What counts as a duplicated block (all conditions required)
A flaggable duplicate is TWO OR MORE added blocks, each NON-TRIVIAL, that are near-identical in structure and differ only in a small set of literals/identifiers/types — i.e. a copy-paste that an Extract Function / shared constant / loop / table would collapse (Fowler, Extract Function; Hunt & Thomas, DRY; Martin). NON-TRIVIAL means the repeated unit is a coherent multi-line computation, validation, mapping/transform, or constant/config block — as a floor, each repeated block spans at least 3 added logical lines (ignore blank lines and lone braces) OR is a repeated literal block of 5+ identical added entries. Blocks may live in the SAME file or across files in this diff. To flag, you MUST quote BOTH occurrences by their [L<n>] spans (and path) and name the small delta between them in one phrase.

## Not duplication (conservative exclusions — apply before flagging)
Do NOT flag any of: a single occurrence (no second block); trivial one-liners, getters/setters, single-statement guard clauses, or import/field declarations; boilerplate the language/framework forces to look alike (equals/hashCode/toString, builder chains, DTO field assignments, switch/enum arms, route or dependency-injection registrations); test code — test methods, fixtures, table-driven cases, arrange/act/assert scaffolding, and parametrized-case bodies are LEGITIMATELY parallel and are never duplication here; generated/vendored files (*.gen.*, *.pb.*, lockfiles, dist/, OpenAPI specs, router/route trees, DB migrations) — assess only the hand-written remainder; and genuinely-parallel cases whose similarity is incidental rather than copy-paste (two unrelated 3-line blocks that merely share a common idiom). When in doubt about whether two blocks are a copy-paste or a coincidental idiom, treat them as NOT duplication.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Deterministic on the count of duplicated COPIES of the worst single block (the number of near-identical occurrences of one block introduced in this diff). 2 copies of one non-trivial block -> MINOR (extract the shared logic once). COSMETIC CAP: near-identical UI/markup/view blocks that carry NO logic or correctness surface (e.g. three SwiftUI text-field rows differing only by label/binding) are a MINOR extract-a-component nudge regardless of count — MAJOR is reserved for duplicated LOGIC/correctness (a validation, a calculation, an error path) where a divergent copy is a real bug risk. 3 or more copies of the same block, OR 2-or-more distinct duplicated blocks each repeated -> MAJOR (the copy-paste is becoming a pattern; collapse it before it spreads). Never exceed MAJOR. Identical added code must always yield identical severity. (The 2-copy MINOR / 3+-copy or multi-block MAJOR cutoffs are catalogue-chosen operational thresholds for consistency, not values reported in the cited literature.)

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote at least two [L<n>] spans (with their path) that are the near-identical added blocks, and name the small delta between them; a duplication claim you cannot ground in two quoted added spans is not made. Never count a block you cannot quote. If the only candidate repetition is in excluded surface (tests, generated/vendored, boilerplate the language forces), do not flag it — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert that the duplicated code is buggy, will diverge, or will break — assess only the observable copy-paste habit.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when a quotable pair of near-identical added blocks is present. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE — including when the diff adds clean non-duplicated logic (the baseline, not a GOOD endorsement), when it is docs-only / config-only / dominated by generated/vendored/test files with no hand-written multi-line logic, OR when the only added code is single occurrences with no repeated block to compare. Guidance is feed-forward: name the one most consequential duplicated block and show the SHAPE of the fix (extract a shared function / introduce a loop over a small table / hoist a named constant) in one or two lines — never rewrite their whole change.

## Observations
- PRESENT, BAD: The diff introduces two or more near-identical non-trivial blocks that a shared function, loop, or constant would collapse — both occurrences quoted by their [L<n>] spans and path, with the small delta named. Graded by severity: MINOR for one block duplicated twice, MAJOR for the same block repeated three-or-more times or for two-or-more distinct duplicated blocks. Pulling the repeated logic into one place would mean the next change touches it once instead of every copy.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The diff is docs-only or config-only, is dominated by generated/vendored/test files with no hand-written multi-line logic to assess, or adds only single non-repeated blocks so there is no near-identical pair to compare for duplication.
practices.whyItMatters: Copy-pasted logic means every future fix has to land in every copy, and the one you forget becomes the bug. Pull the shared part into one place and you fix it once.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: You notice the new validation block matches one a few files over, so you lift it into a single named helper both call, leaving one source of truth instead of two copies that drift apart.

practices.name: Keep functions small and single-purpose
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect — a single added function that sprawls across many responsibilities or buries its core action under deep nesting. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds function bodies but none is overlong/multi-responsibility/deeply-nested. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health asserting 'this function is focused and single-purpose', a whole-function correctness/cohesion judgement you cannot stand behind from the diff alone (the function's collaborators and the responsibilities it delegates to live outside the added lines). Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable overlong/sprawling/deeply-nested added body) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable offending body, or no surface). Assess whether the change decomposes its added/heavily-modified work into small, cohesive, intent-revealing units, or whether it grows a single function that sprawls across many responsibilities or buries logic under deep nesting — framed as a craft habit about how the work is shaped, never as a defect claim about whether the code is correct, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read the actual code in diff.patch and judge the observable shape of the function bodies the change adds; cite the exact added lines by their [L<n>] new-side markers. Judge the HABIT, not a line-by-line lint sweep.

## What this practice judges (and what it does NOT)
Judge ONE thing: is the largest function the change introduces or substantially rewrites a focused unit, or an overlong/multi-responsibility/deeply-nested one? A function shows MIXED RESPONSIBILITIES when one body visibly interleaves separable concerns — e.g. argument parsing AND I/O (network/file/DB) AND business rules AND output formatting — that a reader would naturally name as distinct steps. To count as 'mixes two responsibilities' the two concerns must EACH be SUBSTANTIAL (a multi-step block each, not a 2-4-line serialize/encode/respond tail) AND genuinely INTERLEAVED in the body. A linear pipeline of single-purpose stages — guard → build → encode → return, or validate → operation → respond — is ONE coherent purpose, not two mixed responsibilities, even when the final stage serializes or encodes the result: a short serialize/encode tail after the core action is part of the same pipeline, never a second substantial responsibility. The separable work must be written OUT in the body to count: a thin body that DELEGATES each step to a named collaborator in a single call (parse → fetch → render) is ONE orchestration purpose, not mixed responsibilities — the responsibilities live inside the named helpers, not interleaved in this body, so counting one-line delegated calls as separate concerns is exactly the false positive to avoid. It shows DEEP NESTING when control flow stacks several levels of conditionals/loops (incidental complexity Ousterhout calls obscurity) such that the core action sits far inside the indentation. It is OVERLONG when a single added body runs long enough that its purpose is no longer scannable at a glance. Use judgement informed by McConnell, Ousterhout (deep modules; complexity = obscurity + dependency), Fowler, and Martin — NOT a hard line count, and never quote or imply a line/responsibility threshold to the developer.

## Non-overlap (mandatory — do not double-flag a sibling)
This practice does NOT judge duplication: repeated/near-identical blocks are the concern of removes-duplication — do not raise them here. It does NOT judge dead code, commented-out blocks, or leftover scaffolding: those belong to leaves-code-clean. If the ONLY shape problem you can point to is duplication or dead/commented code, this practice is NOT_APPLICABLE — defer to the sibling. Flag here ONLY genuine size/cohesion/nesting of a single added function body.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the specific [L<n>] span(s) of the offending function body and NAME the separable responsibilities to split out (or the nesting to flatten). A finding you cannot anchor to a quoted added hunk is not emittable — emit NOT_APPLICABLE instead of asserting an unquoted sprawl. Assess only added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context; never reason about a function whose body is not visible in the diff (e.g. an unchanged caller, or a function only renamed/called but not shown). If diff.patch is dominated (>=70% of added lines) by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, snapshots, DB migrations, OpenAPI specs), assess only the hand-written remainder; if essentially nothing hand-written remains, or the diff is docs-only/config-only/pure-deletion/pure-rename with no added function body to judge, emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Grade the SINGLE most consequential offending function (do not sum across files). MINOR = one added/rewritten function is overlong OR moderately deeply nested OR mixes two separable responsibilities, but a reader can still follow it — a nudge to extract. MAJOR = one added/rewritten function clearly does it all (three or more interleaved responsibilities such as parse + I/O + business rule + format) OR nests so deeply that the core action is obscured, materially raising the cost to read and change it. Never exceed MAJOR on function shape alone, and never escalate by counting multiple separate small functions — many small focused units is the baseline (NOT_APPLICABLE), not a defect. Identical diffs must always yield identical observations.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when the diff contains at least one added/substantially-modified function body that is overlong, multi-responsibility, or deeply-nested and you can quote it. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE — including when the added bodies are focused (the baseline, not a GOOD endorsement), when there is no added/substantially-modified function body to judge, or when the only shape concern is duplication or dead/commented code a sibling owns. Guidance is feed-forward: name the one most consequential extraction (e.g. 'pull the request-parsing block at [L<a>]-[L<b>] into its own helper so this function is left with the business rule') and show the shape of the split, never rewrite the whole function.

## Observations
- PRESENT, BAD: One added/rewritten function is overlong, interleaves separable responsibilities, or nests deeply enough to obscure its core action (quote the offending [L<n>] span and name the responsibilities/steps to split out or the nesting to flatten) — graded MINOR when a reader can still follow it (a single extra responsibility or moderate nesting) and MAJOR when it does it all (three or more interleaved concerns) or buries the core action under deep nesting. Extracting the named block into its own intent-revealing helper would leave this function with a single purpose.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The diff carries no added/substantially-modified function body to judge — it is docs-only, config-only, a pure deletion, a pure rename, or dominated by generated/vendored files — or the only shape concern is duplication (removes-duplication) or dead/commented code (leaves-code-clean), so single-purpose function shape cannot be assessed here.

## NA counter-example (do not invent a defect)
A short handler that runs validate → operation → respond with a single try/catch is ONE coherent purpose and is NOT deeply nested — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never a PRESENT, BAD 'mixes concerns' finding. The same holds for a thin handler that delegates each step to a named helper in one line (e.g. filters.fromRequest(req) → repository.findMatching(filters) → renderer.render(rows)): a sequence of one-line delegated calls is one coherent orchestration, never a 'mixes concerns' finding. Flag only a genuinely long (well past the line bucket) or deeply nested (3+ levels) added function.
practices.whyItMatters: A function that does five things is hard to name, hard to test, and hard to change without breaking a part you forgot it touched. Small, focused functions are the ones you can actually hold in your head.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Instead of one long method that parses, validates, saves, and notifies, you have a few short functions, each doing one clearly named step you can read and test on its own.

practices.name: Leave the code clean and comment the why
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect — leftover residue on added lines (commented-out code, debug output, unexplained scaffolding, or a WHAT-only comment). The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the added lines carry none of those blemishes (B == 0). A 'no blemishes, the code is clean' GOOD endorsement is an unverifiable clean bill of health: residue you did not happen to see (a stray print outside the quoted hunks, a leftover in an unread file) cannot be ruled out, and an intent-revealing comment being present elsewhere is not this practice's observation. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable blemish on an added line) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable blemish, or no surface). Assess whether the ADDED lines in this change ship clean — no dead/commented-out code, no leftover debug output, no unexplained scaffolding — and whether any comment that IS present earns its place by explaining WHY (intent, a non-obvious constraint, a tradeoff) rather than restating the WHAT the very next line already says. Framed as a craft habit about what the author leaves behind in the code, never as a correctness or compile claim, and never exceeding MAJOR on this practice alone. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY lines the diff marks as ADDED (a leading '+', carrying the [L<n>] new-side marker); removed ('-') lines and unchanged context are NOT graded — code that was commented out by the author and DELETED in this diff is a cleanup and must never be flagged. Quote the exact [L<n>] span for every blemish you count, with the file path from the hunk header.

## Scope of this practice (non-overlap — enforce so a sibling is never double-flagged)
This practice judges ONLY three surfaces on added lines: (1) commented-out code, (2) leftover debug output, (3) WHAT-only comments. It does NOT judge naming, identifier quality, function or file size, scope/batching, duplication, dead branches in live code, swallowed errors, or any runtime defect — those are sibling or out-of-catalogue practices. If your only concern is one of those, this practice is NOT_APPLICABLE for it; do not borrow another practice's complaint.

## What counts as a blemish (language-agnostic; added lines only)
COMMENTED-OUT CODE: an added comment line whose stripped content is itself plausible source — an assignment, a call with parentheses, a control keyword (if/for/while/return/import/def/func/class/public/const/let), a closing brace, or a block of such lines behind // /* # -- <!-- */. A prose sentence inside a comment is NOT commented-out code even if it names a symbol. DEBUG LEFTOVERS: an added bare diagnostic-output or breakpoint statement with no surrounding handling — console.log / console.debug, print( / println / printf used as a trace, System.out.println, fmt.Println used as a trace, dbg!(, debugger;, var_dump, pp(, a TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK/'remove this'/'temporary' marker left in shipped code, or a commented-out logging line. Do NOT count: a logger call through a real logging facility (log./logger./slf4j/logging.getLogger/zap/winston), output that is the file's deliberate purpose (a CLI's own stdout, a generator's emit, a test's expected-output assertion), or a TODO that an existing project convention clearly sanctions and that references a tracked issue. UNEXPLAINED SCAFFOLDING: an added throwaway placeholder shipped as if final — 'foo'/'bar'/'asdf' stub bodies, a hardcoded 'TEMP'/'REMOVE BEFORE MERGE' value, an empty catch left with a bare debug print. WHAT-ONLY COMMENT: an added comment whose content merely paraphrases the single statement immediately below it ('// increment i' above i++, '// set name to user' above name = user, '// return the result' above return result, '// loop over items' above a for) and adds no intent, constraint, or rationale the code does not already carry. A comment that gives a reason, a unit, a caveat, a why-not, a reference, or a non-obvious invariant is GOOD and must NOT be counted — quote it as evidence of the clean habit, never as a blemish. When unsure whether a comment restates or explains, treat it as explaining (do not flag).

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let A = the number of added (+) NON-generated/vendored lines in diff.patch, and B = the number of distinct blemishes you found on those lines by the rules above (count each offending [L<n>] span once; a contiguous commented-out block counts as ONE blemish, not per line). Quote every blemish span you counted and state A and B. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files — lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, OpenAPI specs, route/router trees, dist/ build output, snapshots, DB migrations — exclude those files entirely and count A and B only over the hand-written remainder.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
B == 0 -> NOT_APPLICABLE (no residue blemish on the added lines — this is the clean baseline, not a craft act to praise; do NOT emit a 'code is clean' GOOD endorsement). B >= 1 with at most ONE blemish AND it is a single WHAT-only comment or a single TODO/marker -> INFO (one stray line to tidy; the gentlest nudge). B >= 1 not meeting that INFO carve-out, up to 3 blemishes -> MINOR (a few leftovers — commented-out code or debug output — slipped into the change). B > 3 blemishes, OR any shipped a debug print left inside an otherwise-empty catch/except (a swallowed error masquerading as handling — functional impact) -> MAJOR. Commented-out code, leftover scaffolding, and WHAT-only comments are MINOR by definition — cosmetic residue with NO functional impact; line count NEVER raises severity here, a block of dead code is a MINOR cleanliness nudge regardless of how many consecutive lines. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice. Identical added lines must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If diff.patch is absent or empty, or every added line lies in generated/vendored files (so A == 0 after exclusion), emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no hand-written added surface on which this habit is observable. Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the exact [L<n>] span(s) and file path of each blemish counted; a blemish you cannot quote is not counted. Never claim a line is dead, unreachable, or debug-only unless the quoted text itself shows it (a commented-out statement, a bare print, a TODO marker) — do not infer intent you cannot see.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when a quotable blemish on an added line exists (A >= 1 after generated/vendored exclusion, B >= 1).
