Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE — including when the added lines are blemish-free (B == 0, the clean baseline), when no hand-written added surface remains, or when the only concern belongs to a sibling. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential leftover (the block to delete, the print to drop, or the one comment to turn from a WHAT into a WHY) and show the shape of the fix — e.g. delete the commented-out lines, or replace '// increment count' with a one-line note on the constraint that makes the increment necessary — never rewrite the whole change.

## Observations
- PRESENT, BAD: At least one added line is a blemish (B >= 1 of A, each [L<n>] span and file quoted) — graded by severity: INFO for a single stray WHAT-only comment or lone TODO, MINOR for up to three leftovers, MAJOR for more than three or a shipped 3+-line commented-out block or a debug print inside an empty catch. Deleting the quoted leftover (or turning the quoted WHAT comment into a note on WHY the code is the way it is) keeps the next reader from sifting residue from intent.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: diff.patch is absent/empty, or every added line is in generated/vendored files so no hand-written added surface remains, or the only concern is naming / size / duplication / a runtime defect that is assessed by a different check — so this clean-code-and-intent habit cannot be assessed here.
practices.whyItMatters: Stray debug output, blocks of commented-out code, and comments that just echo the line all add noise the next reader has to wade through. Clean code with a comment that explains the why reads like someone meant it.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: No leftover print statements or commented-out branches, and where a line looks surprising, a short comment gives the reason, such as 'retry once: the upstream API drops the first call after an idle period', rather than restating what the line does.

areas.name: Handling failure well
areas.description: Fail safely when things go wrong. Handle errors instead of swallowing them, check inputs and edge cases where data enters, and do not crash on a problem the code could recover from.

practices.name: Handle errors instead of swallowing them
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether the failure paths this change INTRODUCES keep their diagnostic context and surface the failure to the caller, instead of silently swallowing it — framed as a craft habit about how errors are handled in the added code, never as a line-by-line lint sweep, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY error-handling constructs that this diff ADDS or MODIFIES (lines carrying an [L<n>] new-side marker); never grade pre-existing code that merely appears as unchanged context, and never assume code outside the diff. Quote the exact added hunk ([L<n>] span) as evidence for every finding.

## What this practice covers (and what it does NOT)
IN SCOPE: an added error-handling construct that DISCARDS the failure — an empty or no-op catch/except/rescue block (e.g. `catch (e) {}`, `except: pass`, `rescue nil`, an empty `catch` that only `break`s); a caught-and-ignored error where the bound error variable is never logged, rethrown, wrapped, or returned (`catch (e) { return null; }`, `catch { /* ignore */ }`); a swallowed async rejection (`.catch(() => {})`, an awaited call wrapped in a bare try whose catch does nothing, a floating promise whose rejection is dropped); a discarded error return (Go `_ = doThing()` or `val, _ := f()` on a function whose second value is `error`; a checked exception or `Result`/`Either` thrown away); or a log-and-continue that ONLY logs at debug/trace and then proceeds as if the call succeeded, hiding a real failure from the caller or UI. NON-OVERLAP (do NOT double-flag a sibling): this practice does NOT judge whether INPUTS are validated or edge cases checked — that is validates-inputs-and-edge-cases-at-the-boundary; and it does NOT judge a DELIBERATE crash/abort on an unrecoverable state (`panic`, `fatalError`, `process.exit`, `throw` to fail fast, `!`/`try!`) — that is avoids-unsafe-panics-and-chosen-crashes. A finding here is ONLY for a failure path that is caught/received and then SILENTLY DROPPED. If the added construct propagates (rethrows, wraps-and-rethrows, returns/forwards the error), records it (logs at warn/error with the error, increments a failure metric, sets an error state the UI reads), or recovers with a documented intentional fallback whose comment names WHY the error is safe to absorb, it is handled — NOT_APPLICABLE, not a finding.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false or diff.patch is absent/empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — the diff could not be materialized; never treat a missing diff as no error handling. If, after excluding generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, OpenAPI specs, route trees, DB migrations, snapshots), the hand-written remainder ADDS no error-handling construct at all — no catch/except/rescue, no `if err != nil`, no `.catch`, no error-returning call whose result is discarded — emit NOT_APPLICABLE; this practice has no surface on a diff that introduces no failure path. Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) it judged. A 'swallowed' finding requires the quoted hunk to SHOW the discard (the empty block body, the `_` assignment, the no-op `.catch`) — if you cannot point to the dropped error in a quoted added line, you do not have a finding.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Let S = the count of DISTINCT added error-handling constructs that swallow a failure by the rules above (each anchored to its own quoted [L<n>] span). S == 0 with at least one added error-handling construct that is properly handled/propagated -> NOT_APPLICABLE. S == 1 on a non-critical path (a log-and-continue that at least records the error at info or higher, or a single ignored error in a clearly best-effort/cleanup path such as a teardown, cache warm, or optional telemetry call) -> MINOR. S == 1 on a consequential path (the swallowed failure hides a real fault from the caller or user — a discarded write/persist/network/auth/parse result, an empty catch around core logic, a dropped promise rejection on a primary flow) OR S >= 2 anywhere -> MAJOR. Never exceed MAJOR. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity; when a construct is ambiguous between best-effort and consequential and the diff carries no comment naming intent, treat it as consequential only if the swallowed call's name or context names a write/persist/network/auth/parse operation, else MINOR.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when the deciding added construct is quotable from diff.patch (enriched=true). Otherwise NOT_APPLICABLE. Judge the HABIT, not every line: name the single most consequential swallowed failure, not an exhaustive list. Guidance is feed-forward and shows the SHAPE of the fix on that one construct (e.g. rethrow with context, log the error at warn/error, return the error to the caller, or add a comment stating why it is intentionally absorbed) — never rewrite the whole change.

## Observations
- NOT_APPLICABLE (handled baseline): Every error-handling construct this diff adds keeps the failure visible — it propagates the error (rethrows, wraps with context, or returns it), records it at warn/error with the error in hand, or absorbs it with an in-diff comment naming why that is safe (S = 0). This is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse.
- PRESENT, BAD: The diff adds at least one failure path that is silently dropped (S >= 1, each quoted from its [L<n>] span) — an empty/no-op catch, a caught-and-ignored error, a discarded error return (`_ =` / `, _ :=`), or a no-op `.catch` — so the caller or UI cannot tell the operation failed. Graded by severity: MINOR for a single best-effort or merely-logged-too-quietly case, MAJOR for a swallowed write/persist/network/auth/parse failure or two-or-more swallows. Show the one most consequential construct and the shape of surfacing its error (rethrow with context, log it, or return it).
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or diff.patch is absent/empty, OR the hand-written remainder (after excluding generated/vendored files) adds no error-handling construct or error-returning call at all, so this practice has no failure-path surface to assess. (Input-validation gaps and deliberate fail-fast crashes are out of scope here — they are assessed by other checks — so do not raise this practice for them.)
practices.whyItMatters: An empty catch block turns a failure into a silent one: the code carries on as if nothing went wrong, and you find out only when something downstream breaks with no clue why. Surfacing the error saves the 2am debugging session.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A failed call logs what went wrong and passes the failure up to a caller who can react, instead of catching the exception and quietly continuing with bad or missing data.

practices.name: Validate inputs and edge cases at the boundary
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether new or changed code that CONSUMES external or cross-boundary input guards the obvious failure cases instead of assuming a happy path — framed as a defensive-programming habit about how the change treats input it does not control (McConnell, Code Complete, defensive programming at module boundaries; SEI CERT input-validation guidance; the software-robustness literature). Read diff.patch and judge ONLY added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context (each changed line carries an [L<n>] new-side marker). Never assume code outside the diff, never claim to run/compile/test it, and never assert a missing guard you cannot point to in a quoted hunk. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice alone.

## What counts as a boundary-consuming site
A boundary site is an ADDED or MODIFIED line where the code reads a value it does not control and then USES it: an HTTP request param/path-var/query/header or a deserialized request body; a parsed string or number (parseInt/Integer.parseInt/Number()/atoi/strconv/JSON.parse and friends); a value pulled from a Map/dict/collection by key or by index; a file/stream read; an environment variable or config lookup; the result of a third-party/library/RPC call. The site is in-scope only when the SAME hunk also DEREFERENCES, INDEXES, ARITHMETICS, or otherwise consumes that value (e.g. calls a method on it, subscripts it, passes it to logic that assumes a shape). A value that is merely passed straight through to another function untouched is NOT a consuming site here. ANCHOR to the precompute: the edge-candidate sites in the precompute hints ARE your in-scope set — evaluate each by file:line, but do NOT invent a consuming site the precompute did not surface (a value forwarded into a request body / SDK / RPC, or member access on a typed value whose shape the type already guarantees, is NOT one).

## The obvious guards (judge presence, not exhaustiveness)
For a consuming site, the obvious-case guard is one of: a null/None/nil/undefined check or an Optional/null-safe access before dereference; an empty/blank/missing-key check before use; a try/catch or error-return around a parse that can throw/fail; a bounds/length check before indexing or slicing; a numeric range/sign/finite check before arithmetic or allocation that assumes a band; or a default/fallback for an absent env/config value. Presence of a framework-level guard VISIBLE IN THE SAME HUNK counts — a Bean Validation annotation (@NotNull/@NotBlank/@Min/@Size/@Valid) on the added param or DTO field, a required-field schema check, a guard clause, or an early return/throw on the bad case. Judge the HABIT (is the obvious failure case handled at all), not a line-by-line lint sweep of every conceivable input.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Let C = the number of distinct added/modified boundary-consuming sites you identify in diff.patch, and U = the number of those whose obvious-case guard is absent in the same hunk or its immediate in-diff context. Quote the [L<n>] span of every site you counted in U and name the specific missing guard (e.g. \"null check before.name\", \"bounds check before [idx]\", \"catch around Integer.parseInt\"). State C and U.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ROBUSTNESS against malformed/absent/out-of-range input only. It does NOT judge security taint-to-sink — if the concern is that untrusted input reaches a dangerous sink unescaped (SQL/HTML/shell/path injection), that belongs to validates-and-escapes-untrusted-input; do NOT flag it here. It does NOT judge error handling/propagation quality — if input IS guarded but the error is swallowed, logged-and-ignored, or surfaced poorly, that belongs to handles-errors; here a try/catch that merely PREVENTS the unguarded crash satisfies the guard regardless of what the catch body does. When a hunk's only defect is escaping or error-propagation, abstain (NOT_APPLICABLE) rather than co-flag.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Buckets on U (unguarded consuming sites): U == 0 -> NOT_APPLICABLE (every introduced boundary site already guards its obvious failure case, so the defect this detector exists to catch is absent — abstain, do NOT issue a reassuring GOOD endorsement on a happy path whose correctness you cannot verify). U == 1 -> MINOR (one added consuming site assumes a happy path). U >= 2 -> MAJOR (multiple introduced sites consume input with no obvious-case guard, so a malformed or absent input crashes or misbehaves on the new path). Single-site escalation: a lone U == 1 site is capped at MINOR and never raised on this practice alone. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If diff.patch is absent/empty, or the change touches no boundary-consuming site (C == 0), emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no input surface this practice applies to; never grade an empty or guard-irrelevant diff as a PRESENT, GOOD strength. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, snapshots, DB migrations, OpenAPI specs), assess ONLY the hand-written remainder; if nothing hand-written consumes boundary input, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. A PRESENT, BAD finding REQUIRES quoting the exact [L<n>] span of each unguarded site and naming its missing guard; there is no GOOD endorsement here — when every consuming site is guarded (U == 0) the defect is absent and you emit NOT_APPLICABLE, not a clean bill of health on logic you cannot execute. Never assert a guard is missing on the strength of code you cannot see — if the value is consumed in the same hunk but a guard could plausibly sit on an unchanged line just above the visible context, treat the site as guarded and do not flag it.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when at least one UNGUARDED boundary-consuming site (U >= 1) is quotable from diff.patch; in every other case — no boundary site (C == 0), or boundary sites present but all guarded (U == 0) — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. There is no GOOD endorsement for this defect-detector. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential unguarded site and show the SHAPE of the guard to add (e.g. \"reject or default the value before line N: if it is null/blank, return early\" or \"wrap the parse so a malformed value yields a handled result, not a thrown one\").

## Observations
- PRESENT, BAD: At least one added/modified site consumes external or cross-boundary input and then dereferences, indexes, or computes on it with no obvious-case guard in the same hunk (U >= 1 of C, each quoted by its [L<n>] span with the missing guard named) — graded by severity: MINOR for a single unguarded site, MAJOR for two or more. Adding the one missing guard at the most consequential site — a null/empty check, a catch around the parse, or a bounds/range check before use — would keep a malformed or absent input from breaking the new path.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: diff.patch is absent/empty, the change consumes no external or cross-boundary input (C == 0), or only generated/vendored files remain after exclusion, so there is no boundary input surface to assess; also NOT_APPLICABLE when a hunk's only concern is escaping untrusted input toward a sink (a sibling security practice) or the quality of error handling once input is already guarded (a sibling error-handling practice).
practices.whyItMatters: Data from a user, a file, or another service will not always arrive in the shape you expected: it can be empty, null, negative, or huge. Checking it where it enters means a clear rejection instead of a confusing crash three layers deep.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Code that reads an external value first asks what happens if it is missing or out of range and handles that on purpose, so an empty list or a malformed payload meets a clean guard instead of a null-pointer surprise.

practices.name: Avoid crashing on recoverable problems
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether the ADDED code degrades gracefully on ordinary runtime failure rather than choosing to crash — framed as a defensive-programming habit (SEI CERT ERR-series; language safety guides on force-unwrap and panic), never as a line-by-line lint sweep, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch and judge ONLY added/modified lines (each carries an [L<n>] new-side marker); never reason about removed lines or code outside the diff, and never claim to compile or run it.

## What a chosen crash is (language-agnostic, added lines only)
A chosen crash is a construct on an added line that deliberately aborts the process on a condition that an ordinary program could recover from. Scan added [L<n>] lines for: force-unwrap / force-cast of a nullable or optional on a value that is NOT a local just-proven invariant (Swift `x!`, `as!`, `try!`; Kotlin `!!`; TS non-null `!` only when it guards a genuine runtime-absent value); a deliberate abort (`fatalError`, `preconditionFailure`, `abort()`, Rust `panic!`/`unwrap()`/`expect()`, Go `panic(`, `log.Fatal`/`log.Panic`, `os.Exit`/`process.exit`/`System.exit`/`exit(`); or a throw raised from a getter/computed-property/`Equatable`/`Hashable`/`Comparable` or other contract-bound accessor where the caller cannot catch it. The deciding line MUST sit on an ORDINARY-runtime path: a value parsed from input, fetched from network/DB/disk/env, decoded from JSON, returned by a fallible API, or any data not constructed three lines above under the same proof. Quote the exact [L<n>] span for the construct AND name the runtime source of the value it crashes on.

## Legitimate invariant (acceptable — not a chosen crash)
Do NOT flag: an assertion/precondition documenting a programmer invariant that cannot hold if surrounding code is correct (a switch over an exhaustive enum's `default` that is genuinely unreachable; `precondition` on a constant or a value proven non-nil on an immediately preceding added line); `process.exit`/`System.exit` in an added CLI `main`/entry-point or a startup/bootstrap fail-fast where aborting IS the contract; force-unwrap of a literal or compile-time-constant resource the author controls (a bundled asset, a hard-coded regex, `URL(string: \"https://...\")!` on a literal). When the crash is a true invariant guard by these tests, treat it as NOT_APPLICABLE — graceful-by-design — not a PRESENT, BAD finding. Also do NOT flag a force-unwrap of an operation that is TOTAL by language contract for its operand type — one that provably cannot fail (return nil / Err) for ANY value of that type — even when the operand is an ordinary-runtime value; totality, not the operand's source, decides this. A canonical case is encoding a String to its UTF-8 bytes (`String.data(using: .utf8)!`), which never returns nil for any String. This allowance is narrow: it does NOT cover fallible conversions where nil/failure is reachable for some inputs (decoding bytes back to a String via `String(data:encoding:)`, encoding with a non-total encoding such as `.ascii`/`.isoLatin1`, dictionary/array subscripts, numeric or URL parses) — those forced on an ordinary-runtime value remain chosen crashes graded by severity as above.

## Non-overlap (do not double-flag a sibling)
Judge ONLY the chosen-crash construct. Do NOT flag a swallowed/empty catch, a logged-and-continued error, or a missing-input-validation gap — those are assessed by other checks and are out of scope here (error swallowing; input validation). If the added line catches an error and then re-crashes on it, the crash is yours; the swallow is not. Stay on the deliberate-abort surface only.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an added chosen crash on an ordinary-runtime value (parsed/fetched/decoded/env/fallible-API) in non-test, non-entry-point hand-written code — a recoverable failure is converted into a process abort. MINOR = an added chosen crash on a path whose recoverability is plausible but not certain from the hunk (e.g. a force-unwrap of a dictionary lookup or an array subscript whose key/index is not literal but whose source you cannot fully pin in-diff). INFO = a single force-unwrap on a borderline-but-likely-safe value where graceful handling would still read better. NOT_APPLICABLE (no severity) = the added code on every fallible path returns/throws-catchably/falls back rather than aborting, OR every abort present is a legitimate invariant guard by the test above. Never exceed MAJOR. Identical added hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the exact [L<n>] crash construct AND name the runtime source of the value it crashes on (where it came from in the same or an adjacent added hunk); a crash you cannot tie to a runtime-sourced value is NOT groundable — do not flag it. If diff.patch is dominated (>=90% of added lines) by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, dist/, snapshots, migrations), assess only the hand-written remainder; if no hand-written added lines carry any of the listed constructs, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. A docs-only, config-only, or pure-data diff with no added code carries no surface for this practice — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never infer a crash from a removed line or from code the diff does not show.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when added code lines are present and you can quote the deciding [L<n>] span. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential chosen crash, show the shape of the graceful alternative for THAT line (guard-let / if-let / `try?` with a fallback / return-an-error / Result), and never rewrite the whole change.

## Observations
- NOT_APPLICABLE (graceful baseline): Every added fallible path degrades gracefully — values from input/network/DB/decode are unwrapped safely (guard/if-let, default, catchable throw, Result) — or every abort present is a legitimate invariant guard (unreachable enum default, precondition on a value proven on a preceding line, entry-point fail-fast, force-unwrap of an author-controlled literal). This is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse.
- PRESENT, BAD: An added line chooses to crash on an ordinary-runtime value (quote the [L<n>] force-unwrap / try! / fatalError / panic! / process.exit / getter-throw AND name where the value came from) where the failure was recoverable — graded by severity: MAJOR when the recoverable runtime source is clear, MINOR when recoverability is plausible but not certain from the hunk, INFO for a single borderline-but-likely-safe unwrap. Handling that case (guard-let / try? with a fallback / return an error) instead of aborting would let the program survive the failure.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No added line carries any chosen-crash construct, or the diff is docs/config/data-only with no added code, or the only such constructs sit in generated/vendored files, so this habit has no surface to assess.
practices.whyItMatters: Reaching for a force-unwrap or a panic on a condition that can actually happen turns an ordinary hiccup into a hard crash for your users. A problem the code can recover from deserves a graceful path, not a deliberate stop.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Where a value might legitimately be absent, the code unwraps it safely and falls back to a default or a clear error, instead of force-unwrapping and bringing the whole thing down.

areas.name: Making changes secure by default
areas.description: Make each change secure by default. Validate and escape untrusted input before it reaches a dangerous sink, avoid insecure defaults and over-broad permissions, and change dependencies deliberately.

practices.name: Neutralize untrusted input before a dangerous sink
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess whether code ADDED in this diff that carries untrusted input into a dangerous sink neutralizes it — parameterizes, escapes, or validates — rather than concatenating raw input into the sink. Read diff.patch and trace the flow inside the added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context. Frame this as the security craft habit the change demonstrates, never as a line-by-line lint sweep, and never exceeding MAJOR. This practice judges ONLY a taint-to-sink injection flow; it does NOT judge generic input validation/robustness or edge-case handling (that is validates-inputs-and-edge-cases-at-the-boundary) and does NOT judge hardcoded credentials or leaked secrets (that is avoids-insecure-defaults-and-over-broad-permissions) — if the only concern in the diff is a missing bounds/null/format check with no dangerous sink, or a committed secret, this practice is NOT_APPLICABLE here so it never double-flags a sibling.

## What counts as untrusted input (a source)
A value the change reads from outside the trust boundary on the added lines: HTTP request params/query/path variables, headers, cookies, request bodies, multipart/file-upload names or contents, WebSocket/RPC payloads, CLI args, environment variables consumed as data, or a response from a third-party/remote service. A value that is a compile-time constant, a value already validated/escaped earlier IN THE QUOTED HUNK, or an internal trusted identifier is NOT a source.

## What counts as a dangerous sink
Added code that reaches one of: a SQL/NoSQL query (string-built query text), an OS command / shell exec / subprocess, an HTML/template/DOM render or `innerHTML`/`dangerouslySetInnerHTML`/server-side template interpolation, a filesystem path (open/read/write/include), an HTTP redirect/`Location` target, an object-deserialization/`eval`/dynamic-code call, or an LDAP/XPath/header-injection target. The flow must be observable in the diff: the source value must reach the sink on an added line you can quote with its [L<n>] marker.

## Neutralization that makes a flow safe (quote it)
The flow is neutralized when the added code does any of: binds the value as a parameter (prepared-statement placeholder / bound query param, never string-interpolated SQL), uses an ORM/query-builder that parameterizes, escapes for the exact sink context (HTML/attribute/URL encoder, shell-arg quoting/`execFile` with an args array, path canonicalization + allow-list / `Path.resolve` confinement check), validates against an allow-list or strict type/format before the sink, or renders through an auto-escaping template with escaping left on. You MUST quote the [L<n>] line that performs the neutralization to claim a flow is safe.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] hunk where the source reaches the sink unneutralized, name the source, and name the sink — a flow you cannot quote end-to-end (source AND sink both on quoted added lines) is not assertable; if you cannot point to both, do not raise it. Never claim the code runs, compiles, is exploitable, or that a payload triggers — assert only the visible habit (raw input concatenated into a sink with no in-diff neutralization). If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, dist/, migrations, OpenAPI specs), assess only the hand-written remainder; if no hand-written added line carries a source-to-sink flow, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Judge ONLY added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context — never assume code outside the diff sanitizes or fails to sanitize the value.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an added flow concatenates/interpolates untrusted input directly into a SQL/NoSQL query, an OS command/shell, a deserialization/`eval`, or a filesystem path with no quotable neutralization (the classic injection / path-traversal / RCE shapes — OWASP A03, CWE Top 25). MINOR = an added flow reaches an HTML/template/DOM render, a redirect target, or a header/LDAP/XPath sink with no quotable escaping/validation (reflected/stored XSS, open-redirect, header injection). INFO = the value is partially constrained on an added line (e.g. type-cast to a number, length-checked) but the context-correct escape/parameterization for the sink is still missing — a light nudge. NOT_APPLICABLE (no severity) = every added source-to-sink flow in the diff carries a quotable in-diff neutralization. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when at least one untrusted-input-to-dangerous-sink flow is fully quotable on added lines. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential unneutralized flow and show the SHAPE of the fix for that one sink (e.g. switch the interpolated query to a bound parameter, swap `innerHTML` for a text node or an escaping render, replace the shell string with an args-array exec, confine the path under a canonicalized base + allow-list) —.

## Observations
- NOT_APPLICABLE (neutralized baseline): The diff introduces at least one untrusted-input-to-dangerous-sink flow and every such flow is neutralized on a quotable added [L<n>] line (bound parameter, context-correct escape, allow-list/format validation, path confinement, or auto-escaping render), so the change carries untrusted input into the sink safely. This is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse.
- PRESENT, BAD: At least one added flow carries untrusted input into a dangerous sink with no quotable in-diff neutralization — quote the [L<n>] hunk, name the source and the sink, and graded by severity: MAJOR for a SQL/NoSQL/command/deserialization/path-traversal flow, MINOR for an HTML/template/redirect/header/LDAP/XPath flow, INFO when the value is partly constrained but the context-correct escape is still absent. Showing the parameterized/escaped/validated shape for that one sink would close the injection path.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No added line carries a quotable untrusted-input-to-dangerous-sink flow — a docs-only/config-only diff, a change whose only input concern is a generic missing bounds/format check with no dangerous sink (deferred to validates-inputs-and-edge-cases-at-the-boundary), a secrets-only concern (deferred to avoids-insecure-defaults-and-over-broad-permissions), or a diff dominated by generated/vendored files with no hand-written source-to-sink flow in the remainder — so this practice has no surface to assess.
practices.whyItMatters: Gluing raw user input straight into a query, a shell command, or a page is how injection bugs are born, the kind that leak a database or run an attacker's code. Parameterizing or escaping it first is what keeps that door shut.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: User-supplied values reach the database through bound parameters and the page through proper escaping, never concatenated into a SQL string or dropped into HTML as-is.

practices.name: Avoid insecure defaults and over-broad permissions
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds the relevant construct(s) but none exhibit the defect. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable instance of the defect) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable defect, or no surface). Assess, by reading the ADDED lines in diff.patch, whether this change introduces an insecure default or widens a permission/exposure boundary with no stated justification — framed as a security-hygiene habit about the configuration and access posture the change ships, never as a blanket vulnerability scan and never exceeding MAJOR on a single setting. This judges the OWASP A05 (Security Misconfiguration) / A02 (Sensitive-Data Exposure) / A09 (Security Logging) / Microsoft SDL / CIS family: insecure transport/verification toggles, wildcard or credentialed cross-origin exposure, debug/verbose-error toggles shipped to a non-test surface, world-writable / over-permissive file or resource modes, auth/permission guards removed or loosened, a SECRET OR BEARER TOKEN placed in a URL PATH OR QUERY (it leaks into proxy/server/access logs — a token belongs in a header, never the URL), a SECURE-STORAGE write (keychain/keystore, e.g. SecItemAdd) with NO accessibility class set (the default is over-broad: backup-eligible / readable when locked), and LOGGING of a TOKEN / CREDENTIAL / PASSWORD / auth RESPONSE BODY to console or log. ## UNCONDITIONAL SINK SCAN (run FIRST, independently of any precompute list — a missing candidate is NEVER grounds for NA)
Before anything else, scan every ADDED (+) line of diff.patch for these two literal sinks and, if either is present, this practice is PRESENT, BAD — never NOT_APPLICABLE — even when the precompute insecureDefaultCandidates list is empty or absent:
(a) a logging or string-interpolation call (print, NSLog, os_log, Logger.*, console.log, String(data:...), or any "...\(x)..." template) whose interpolated argument derives from an auth/login/token/refresh response body OR from a symbol named *Token / *Secret / *[Pp]assword / *Credential / *ApiKey — quote the exact [L<n>] line and emit PRESENT, BAD at MAJOR (the secret/auth body leaks into console and aggregated logs). Never NA this while such a line is visible.
(b) a SecItemAdd / SecItemUpdate keychain write whose attribute dictionary contains NO kSecAttrAccessible key — quote the exact [L<n>] line and emit PRESENT, BAD at MINOR (the default protection class is over-broad: backup-eligible and readable while the device is unlocked-once). Never NA this while such a line is visible.
The absence of a precompute candidate is NEVER grounds for NA while a sink-(a) or sink-(b) line is in the added diff: you read the diff yourself, you do not depend on the precompute list to find these. The per-practice precompute (read work/precompute-out) ALSO surfaces these as insecureDefaultCandidates with file:line — when present you MUST open and evaluate each flagged site too; do not NA the practice as 'no insecure default' while a precompute candidate sits unexamined on the auth/transport surface, and equally do not NA while an unflagged but visible sink-(a)/sink-(b) line sits in the added diff. Judge ONLY added (and modified) lines and their immediate in-diff context; cite the exact added line(s) by quoting the [L<n>] span.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice does NOT judge whether untrusted input is escaped/validated (that is validates-and-escapes-untrusted-input). It DOES own EXPOSED CREDENTIAL MATERIAL — a secret/token/password/API-key interpolated into a URL or connection string (it leaks into proxy/access logs), or a credential literal assigned to a secret-named field committed to a non-test source path — as an OWASP A02 sensitive-data-exposure misconfiguration; no other practice covers it. The narrow out-of-scope case is a secret-named literal confined to an observable test/sample/fixture file with no exposure surface. You judge the SETTING/POSTURE the change adopts, not the data flowing through it and not the presence of a secret value.

## Patterns in scope (quote the exact [L<n>] added line for any you cite)
(1) Disabled transport security or certificate/host verification — e.g. an added line setting TLS/SSL verification off, accepting all certs/hostnames, `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, a trust-all manager, or downgrading https→http for a non-local target. (2) Over-broad cross-origin exposure — an added CORS config with `*` origin, reflecting arbitrary origins, or `*` combined with credentials-allowed. (3) Debug / verbose-error defaults on a shipped surface — `debug=true`, `DEBUG = True`, stack-trace/verbose-error exposure, dev-only middleware, or a disabled CSRF/security filter introduced on a path that is NOT a test/sample file. (4) Over-permissive resource modes — world-writable or `0777`/`chmod 777`/`FullControl`/public-everyone ACL, a publicly-readable bucket/object, or `permitAll`/auth-disabled on a route. (5) Auth/permission guard removed or loosened — an added/modified line that DELETES or weakens an existing authentication/authorization check (e.g. removing a role/scope check, flipping a guard to allow-all, broadening a required role to a weaker one).

## Stated-justification carve-out (deterministic)
Widening is only a PRESENT, BAD finding when it ships WITHOUT a stated reason. A justification counts ONLY if it is observable in the artifact and you can quote it: an inline code comment on or directly adjacent to the changed line, OR a sentence in metadata.body, that explicitly names why the setting is acceptable here (e.g. 'local-dev only', 'internal mTLS terminates upstream', 'public asset CDN by design'). A justification that is merely plausible but not written down does NOT count — quote it or treat it as absent. A setting confined to an observably test/sample/fixture/example/local file path (filename or directory contains test, spec, fixture, example, sample, or a `*.local.*` / dev-compose marker) is itself a stated context: treat such a change as NOT_APPLICABLE/abstain, not a PRESENT, BAD finding.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an added line disables transport/certificate verification, exposes a wildcard credentialed cross-origin surface, sets world-writable/0777 on a non-test path, removes/loosens an authentication/authorization guard, OR exposes credential material (a secret/token/password/API-key embedded in a URL, a connection string, or committed to a non-test source path) — with NO quotable justification and NOT confined to a test/sample/local path. Exposed credential material is MAJOR regardless of any blast-radius framing. A secret/bearer/refresh token interpolated into a URL PATH OR QUERY, and the logging of a token/credential/password or an auth RESPONSE BODY, are ALWAYS MAJOR — never downgrade either to MINOR on a blast-radius, internal-only, or dev-tooling rationale. MINOR = a lower-blast-radius misconfig with no quotable justification on a non-test path: a non-credentialed wildcard CORS origin, a `debug=true`/verbose-error or disabled-CSRF toggle, or an over-permissive (but not world-writable) mode/ACL. INFO is NOT used by this practice. NOT_APPLICABLE (no severity) = the security-relevant config the diff introduces is safe, OR a widening change carries a quotable justification, OR it is confined to a test/sample/local surface. Never escalate above MAJOR on one setting; if several independent insecure settings appear, name the single most consequential one at its bucket and mention the others in one clause — do not sum severities.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that constitute the insecure default or the removed/loosened guard, name the file path, and state that no quotable justification (inline comment or metadata.body sentence) accompanies it. A removed-guard finding must quote the removed/weakened line from the diff hunk (the `-` line or the before→after pair) — do not assert a guard was removed you cannot point to. A NOT_APPLICABLE that rests on a justification MUST quote that justification verbatim. Never assert a setting is insecure from a file name, a diff_summary count, or a setting you cannot quote; never claim to run/compile/test the config. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, dist/, migrations, OpenAPI specs) assess only the hand-written remainder, and if no hand-written security-relevant config remains, abstain.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, BAD finding only when a quotable added insecure default / permission-widening line is present on a non-test path with no quotable justification. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE (safe/justified/test-confined) when the change actually TOUCHES a security-relevant configuration or guard surface and that surface is safe (or justified, or test-confined) — and equally when the change touches no transport/CORS/debug/file-mode/auth-guard surface at all (e.g. a pure business-logic, docs-only, styling, or dependency-bump diff), or when the only security-relevant artifact is a secret-named literal confined to an observable test/sample/fixture path with no exposure surface. Identical added lines and identical justification context MUST always yield an identical observation.

## Guidance (feed-forward, developer-facing — never name the grade, severity, band, or any threshold)
Name the ONE most consequential setting and show the shape of the safer default, not a rewrite of the change: for disabled verification, point to turning verification back on and pinning/trusting the specific CA instead of trusting all; for wildcard CORS, point to naming the exact allowed origin(s) instead of `*`, and never pairing `*` with credentials; for a debug/verbose toggle, point to gating it behind a dev-only profile; for an over-permissive mode, point to the least-privilege mode that still works; for a removed guard, point to restoring the check or recording, in the body or an inline comment, the reason the route is now open. Talk about the configuration and the next step, never about the contributor.

## Observations
- NOT_APPLICABLE (safe/justified/test-confined baseline): The change touches a security-relevant configuration or access guard and ships it safely — a tightened or correctly-scoped setting, a widening that carries a verbatim-quotable justification (inline comment or metadata.body sentence, quoted), or a setting confined to an observably test/sample/local path. This is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse.
- PRESENT, BAD: An added [L<n>] line introduces an insecure default or widens a permission/exposure boundary on a non-test path with no quotable justification — quote the exact line and path; for a removed/loosened guard quote the removed or weakened line from the hunk. Grade by severity: MAJOR for disabled transport/cert verification, wildcard credentialed CORS, world-writable/0777 on a non-test path, or a removed/loosened auth/authz guard; MINOR for non-credentialed wildcard CORS, a debug/verbose-error or disabled-CSRF toggle, or an over-permissive (non-world-writable) mode/ACL. Name the single most consequential setting and point forward to the safer default.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The hand-written portion of the diff touches no transport/verification, cross-origin, debug/error-exposure, file-mode/ACL, or auth-guard surface AND introduces no exposed credential material AND contains no unconditional-scan sink — no logging/interpolation of an auth-response body or a *Token/*Secret/*[Pp]assword symbol (sink a), and no SecItemAdd/SecItemUpdate-without-kSecAttrAccessible (sink b) — so there is no insecure-default, permission-widening, or secret-exposure posture to assess; or the diff is generated/vendored-dominated with no hand-written security config remaining. A bare committed secret/token/credential is NOT a reason to NA here — this practice is its terminal owner, so report it as PRESENT, BAD at MAJOR under this slug.

## Exposed credential material — report it HERE, under this slug
This practice is the terminal owner of exposed credential material in the diff. A hardcoded secret, credential, API key, token, or password — assigned to a variable on an ADDED line (e.g. a value matching sk-/ghp-/AKIA or assigned to an apiKey/secret/token/password field) or placed into a URL/query/log (e.g. `key=\(apiKey)`) — is a PRESENT, BAD finding at MAJOR reported UNDER THIS PRACTICE'S OWN SLUG (avoids-insecure-defaults-and-over-broad-permissions) — do NOT invent a new, secrets-specific slug for it (this practice's ceiling), even if a secrets-specific sibling practice exists or is not in this run. Quoting a secret literal or token-in-URL and then emitting NOT_APPLICABLE is a forbidden BUG — never defer it to another slug. Guidance for a committed live secret/credential MUST state TWO steps as SEPARATE, explicit clauses and never omit either: (1) remove it from the code (and git history) and load it from a secret store / environment variable, passing secrets in a header not a URL; and (2) ROTATE/revoke the exposed value immediately — once committed it must be treated as compromised. Stating only step (1) is incomplete.
practices.whyItMatters: A wide-open CORS rule, a world-readable file, or a token with far more access than it needs sits quietly until someone exploits it. Starting locked down means you only open what you can justify.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A new endpoint or credential grants exactly the access it needs and no more, with scoped permissions and a specific allowed origin, instead of a permissive default dropped in to make things work.

practices.name: Change dependencies deliberately
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the change touches its dependency manifest/lockfile DELIBERATELY: the lockfile moves in step with the manifest (no orphaned churn in either direction), and a notable new dependency or version bump is EXPLAINED rather than dropped in as an unexplained pin. Framed as a supply-chain workflow habit (OWASP A06 Vulnerable/Outdated Components; reproducible-build practice) about how a dependency edit is justified and kept coherent — never as a code-defect claim about the dependency's own correctness, and never exceeding MAJOR. Judge ONLY the hand-written dependency surface in this diff; do not assert anything about the package's runtime behavior, its known CVEs, or whether the chosen version is the 'right' one.

## Where dependencies live (single authoritative surface)
Read ONLY the manifest and lockfile hunks in diff.patch. MANIFEST files (hand-authored, where a human declares a dependency and its version range): package.json, pom.xml, build.gradle(.kts), go.mod, requirements*.txt / pyproject.toml / Pipfile, Gemfile, Cargo.toml, composer.json, *.csproj. LOCKFILE files (the pinned, usually generated resolution): pnpm-lock.yaml, package-lock.json, yarn.lock, go.sum, poetry.lock / Pipfile.lock, Gemfile.lock, Cargo.lock, composer.lock. Identify the set of changed manifest files (M) and changed lockfile files (L) from the diff_summary.md file-path table, then read their hunks. State which manifest/lockfile paths you read.

## What counts as a deliberate, traceable dependency edit
Two observable sub-checks, judged from the quoted hunks only:
COHERENCE — the manifest and lockfile move together. A manifest dependency line is added/removed/version-changed (quote the [L<n>] line) and the corresponding lockfile shows a matching add/remove/version entry (quote it), OR the change touches only a lockfile in a way that is itself self-consistent (a routine resolution refresh with no manifest edit is coherent, not an orphan). ORPHANED CHURN = a manifest dependency add/bump with NO accompanying lockfile change in this diff when a lockfile for that ecosystem exists and was committed before (quote the unmatched manifest [L<n>] line and state the lockfile was not in the changed-file set), or a brand-new direct dependency appearing only in a lockfile with no manifest declaration. No-lockfile carve-out: if no sibling lockfile for that ecosystem exists anywhere in the repo (the precompute reports lockfilePresent=0 and touched none), there is nothing for the manifest to be out of step with — the manifest edit is COHERENT by absence, never orphaned churn; do NOT raise MAJOR orphaned-churn unless you can name the existing lockfile path that was left out of the changed-file set.
EXPLANATION — a NOTABLE add or bump is justified somewhere observable: the same-file in-diff context, an adjacent comment in the manifest hunk, the PR title/body (metadata.title / metadata.body), or a commit subject in metadata.commits[] that names the dependency or the reason (quote the explaining span verbatim). 'Notable' = a NEW direct RUNTIME/PRODUCTION dependency, or a MAJOR-version bump of one (leading version component changes, e.g. 3.x -> 4.x), or removal of a direct runtime dependency, or the removal of a license/provenance header on vendored source committed in this diff. A patch/minor bump of an existing dependency, or a transitive-only lockfile delta, is NOT notable and never needs prose. DEV-TOOLING CARVE-OUT (suppresses only the ADDITION of dev tooling — NEVER the loosening or removal of a version pin): the carve-out does NOT apply when a dependency edit LOOSENS or DROPS a version pin (e.g. '2.235.0' -> unconstrained, or removing a locked transitive) even on a dev/tooling manifest — un-pinning is a CI-reproducibility regression and is always notable (at least MINOR) because a future build can silently resolve a different version. PRECEDENCE (apply first, deterministically): classify the edit as ADD / REMOVE / PIN-LOOSEN / PIN-DROP / BUMP (the precompute supplies this as dep:<FACT>); a PIN-LOOSEN or PIN-DROP is ALWAYS notable at MINOR and the dev-tooling carve-out NEVER suppresses it, even on a Gemfile/fastlane or any dev/tooling manifest, while the carve-out suppresses ONLY a plain ADD of a dev/tooling dependency — when both could apply (a dev-tooling entry that also loosens or drops a pin), the pin-loosen/pin-drop rule wins. Otherwise: an add to a dev/tooling-only manifest that does not enter the shipped runtime — a Gemfile/Brewfile/.tool-versions entry, a package.json devDependencies/scripts entry, or a dependency declared in a clearly dev/test/CI scope (e.g. a build-plugin, a linter, a formatter, fastlane) — is dev-tooling, not a production supply-chain change. Treat it as non-notable for the orphan/MAJOR ladder: a missing lockfile entry for such an add is at most MINOR (an unexplained pin), never the MAJOR orphaned-churn bucket, because no reproducible RUNTIME artifact depends on it. When you cannot tell from the quoted hunk whether a dependency is runtime or dev-only, treat it as dev-tooling and cap at MINOR rather than escalating. UNEXPLAINED PIN = a notable add/bump with no quotable justification on any of those surfaces.

## Non-overlap (do not double-flag a sibling)
Do NOT judge whether the lockfile/generated churn should have been excluded from review size or committed at all — that is excludes-generated-and-build-artifacts' job. Here a large generated lockfile delta is NORMAL and expected EVIDENCE of coherence, never a finding against this practice. Assess only deliberateness (coherence + explanation), not artifact hygiene or review burden.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = orphaned churn on a notable RUNTIME/PRODUCTION dependency (a new direct runtime dependency or a major-version bump of one in the manifest with no matching lockfile entry, or a new direct runtime dependency surfacing only in the lockfile), OR a removed license/provenance header on vendored source — the resolution is incoherent and the build is not reproducible from the manifest as written. A dev-tooling manifest add (see DEV-TOOLING CARVE-OUT) with no lockfile entry is NOT this bucket; it caps at MINOR. MINOR = manifest and lockfile are coherent but a notable add/bump (new direct dependency, major-version bump, or direct-dependency removal) carries no quotable explanation on any surface — an unexplained pin. INFO = a notable add/bump is coherent and partially explained (named but with no reason, e.g. the commit subject says 'add foo' without why). PRESENT, GOOD (no severity) = manifest+lock are coherent AND every notable add/bump has a quotable why, OR the only dependency delta is non-notable (patch/minor or transitive-only) and coherent. Never exceed MAJOR on dependency deliberateness alone. Identical hunks must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every present element MUST be quoted: quote the manifest [L<n>] dependency line, the matching (or missing) lockfile span, and the explaining sentence/subject verbatim. A COHERENCE claim requires quoting both the manifest change and the lockfile change (or stating, with the file-path table named, that no lockfile was in the changed set). An ORPHAN claim requires quoting the unmatched manifest line AND naming that the ecosystem's lockfile is absent from the changed-file table. An UNEXPLAINED-PIN claim requires quoting the notable manifest add/bump and stating that title, body, the in-hunk context, and commits[] were all scanned and none justifies it. If a dependency file is dominated by generated noise, still judge the manifest line you can quote; do not invent a transitive cause you cannot point to.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when at least one manifest or lockfile file is in the changed set and its deciding hunk is quotable. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. never claim a CVE or vulnerability you cannot read in the diff.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: The dependency edit is deliberate — the manifest change and the lockfile change are quoted moving together (or the only delta is a coherent non-notable/transitive refresh), and every notable add or major bump has a verbatim-quoted reason on the manifest context, PR title/body, or a commit subject, so a reviewer can see WHY the dependency moved and trust the build still resolves.
- assessment=BAD: Either a notable dependency was added or bumped with no quotable explanation anywhere (an unexplained pin — MINOR; INFO when it is named but the why is missing), or the manifest and lockfile are out of step (a manifest add/bump with no matching lockfile entry, or a new direct dependency only in the lockfile — MAJOR, orphaned churn). Quote the offending [L<n>] line; the next step is to regenerate the lockfile alongside the manifest and add one sentence on why this dependency was added or bumped.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No manifest or lockfile file is in the changed-file set (a code-, docs-, or config-only diff with no dependency surface), so there is no dependency edit whose deliberateness could be assessed.
practices.whyItMatters: Every dependency you add is code you now trust and have to keep patched, and a lockfile that drifts from the manifest breaks reproducible builds. A one-line note on why it is there saves a future teammate from guessing whether it is safe to remove.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A new package arrives with a sentence on what it is for, the lockfile updated in the same commit as the manifest, and no leftover version churn in unrelated packages.

areas.name: Recording decisions and documenting changes
areas.description: Capture the why. Record the reasoning behind significant design decisions, and document changes to public APIs and shared behaviour, so the people who maintain or build on the code later can understand it.

practices.name: Record significant decisions and the reasoning
practices.criteria:
Assess whether an architecturally SIGNIFICANT change in this PR carries its WHY — the recorded rationale a future maintainer needs to understand the decision, not just re-read the code — framed as a craft habit about preserving design intent, never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. This practice fires ONLY when the diff contains an architecturally significant decision; it is NOT a general 'describe your PR' check. Two stages: (1) detect significance from diff.patch + diff_summary.md; (2) if and only if significance is present, check whether its rationale is recorded in metadata.title, metadata.body, or metadata.commits[] subjects.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag describe-what-and-why)
The general adequacy of the PR description (a specific title + a why sentence for the change as a whole) belongs to describe-what-and-why and is OUT OF SCOPE here. This practice gates STRICTLY on architectural significance: if the change has no significant decision by the catalogue below, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — even when the body is empty or weak. Conversely, when a significant decision IS present, judge ONLY whether THAT decision's rationale is recorded; do not grade overall title/body quality. A PR can be a PRESENT, GOOD strength here (its significant decision is explained) while still assessment=BAD under describe-what-and-why, or vice versa — the two never share a observation surface.

## Significance catalogue (deterministic — the ONLY surfaces that count)
Treat the change as architecturally significant iff the hand-written portion of diff.patch contains at least one of the following, and you can QUOTE the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that evidence it:
- NEW DEPENDENCY / FRAMEWORK: an added (+) line introducing a new dependency or version-defining declaration in a manifest — pom.xml (<dependency>/<artifactId>), build.gradle(.kts), package.json dependencies, requirements.txt, go.mod require, Cargo.toml [dependencies], Gemfile. A version BUMP of an already-present dependency does NOT count (that is a routine upgrade).
- SCHEMA / DATA-MODEL CHANGE: an added Liquibase/Flyway changeset (createTable, addColumn, dropColumn, addForeignKeyConstraint, renameColumn), a new or altered @Entity / @Table / @Column / @ManyToOne mapping, a CREATE/ALTER TABLE in an SQL file, or a new persistence schema definition.
- PUBLIC-CONTRACT CHANGE: an added/removed endpoint or signature on a public surface — a new @RestController / @GetMapping / @PostMapping / @PatchMapping route, an edit to openapi.yaml paths, a new public method on a service/SPI/interface, a changed DTO/record component on an API type, or a renamed/removed public field.
- CROSS-CUTTING PATTERN / MODULE: a newly added cross-cutting mechanism — a new @Aspect, @RestControllerAdvice, filter/interceptor (@Component implementing Filter/HandlerInterceptor), an event listener (@EventListener / @TransactionalEventListener), a new module package root, a base class many types will extend, or a configuration class wiring a new infrastructure bean.
- AUTH / SECURITY MECHANISM: a SecurityFilterChain edit, an authentication/authorization rule (@PreAuthorize, requestMatchers, permitAll/authenticated), a new token/HMAC/crypto verifier, a CORS/CSRF policy change, or a secret-handling path.
If NONE of these is quotable from the hand-written diff, the change is routine — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Pure additions of tests, formatting, comments, docs, renames-without-contract-change, and version bumps are NOT significant. Tuned literal constants / magic numbers (thresholds, timeouts, debounce counts, retry limits, buffer sizes) added to a private or internal symbol are NOT in the catalogue — they are routine tuning, not an architectural decision; emit NOT_APPLICABLE for them even when the value looks non-obvious. Choosing a specific number is a parameter, not a structural choice this practice governs.

## Generated/vendored exclusion
Detect significance ONLY from hand-written hunks. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, *.pb.*, openapi-CLIENT output under src/api/**, routeTree.gen.*, dist/, snapshots), exclude those from the significance scan: a dependency appearing only in a lock file (with no manifest edit) is the mechanical echo of a manifest change, not an independent decision. A migration changeset under db/changelog/ IS hand-authored schema intent and DOES count as significant (do not treat it as generated).

## Rationale check (only after significance is established)
The WHY is RECORDED iff at least one of these supplies a verbatim-quotable reason for the significant decision: (a) a quotable clause in metadata.body stating the motivation, trade-off, alternative-rejected, or constraint behind the significant change — an ADR-style 'we chose X because Y' / 'to avoid Z', INCLUDING an inline reason-connective clause embedded in a bulleted Implementation-Details list (e.g. 'canonicalized yaw, so it doesnt flip 90 degrees', 'EMA smoothing, so that box moves smoothly'); scan the WHOLE body end to end — every bullet, checklist item, and nested Implementation/Details list — for 'so that' / 'so it' / 'because' / 'to avoid' / 'to prevent' / 'single source of truth ... so ...' before concluding the why is absent (a rationale clause inside a bullet counts exactly as one in prose); (b) a commit subject in metadata.commits[] that pairs the significant change with a reason clause (e.g. 'add Resilience4j to bound downstream call latency'); or (c) a linked ADR / design-doc reference in the body (a path under docs/**/adr or an ADR-#### token or a design-doc URL) that names this decision. To claim the WHY is present you MUST quote BOTH the significant diff line ([L<n>]) AND the exact rationale sentence/subject/reference. A body that only restates WHAT changed, an empty body, an unmodified template, or a bare 'added dependency X' with no reason does NOT record a why. Do NOT invent a rationale the author did not write, and do NOT infer the why from the code itself — the whole point is that the code does not carry it.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
presence=PRESENT with assessment=GOOD = at least one significant decision is detected AND its WHY is recorded (both the diff line and the rationale quoted). MINOR = a significant decision ships with no recorded why and it is a single, contained significance signal (e.g. one new dependency, or one isolated schema column) — a light nudge to add the rationale. MAJOR (LITERAL LOOKUP — a missing why is MAJOR only when at least one of these clauses is satisfied, and the reasoning MUST NAME the exact clause it relied on; if none is satisfied the ceiling is MINOR): the unexplained decision is (i) an auth/security mechanism, OR (ii) a wire/persistence/public-API/public-contract change, OR (iii) a genuine cross-cutting pattern/module the rest of the app plugs into (a base class many types extend, a shared interceptor/aspect/event bus), OR (iv) two or more distinct significance signals from the catalogue co-occur unexplained. A lone app-internal class/view/effect/component, or any single contained signal that fits NONE of clauses (i)-(iv), is MINOR — do NOT default to MAJOR. A MAJOR finding whose reasoning does not name the satisfied clause is invalid; re-grade it MINOR. Never exceed MAJOR on rationale alone. Identical diffs (same quoted significance signals, same body/commit text) must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false, or diff.patch is absent/empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no materialized change to scan for significance; never treat a missing diff as routine or as significant. Every PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) that establish significance; if you cannot quote a significance signal from a hand-written hunk, you may NOT emit an assessment=BAD finding — emit NOT_APPLICABLE instead. A PRESENT, GOOD strength additionally REQUIRES the verbatim rationale sentence/subject/reference quoted. An assessment=BAD finding requires that, after scanning metadata.body AND every metadata.commits[] subject AND the body for an ADR/design-doc reference, none supplies a quotable reason for the quoted significant change (state which surfaces you scanned). Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior, never claim the dependency/schema/endpoint is wrong — judge only whether the decision's WHY is written down.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when a significance signal is quotable from a hand-written hunk (enriched=true, diff present). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward and decision-shaped: name the single most consequential undocumented decision, quote it, and show the shape of the fix — a one-line 'why' to add to the body ('## Why <one sentence: the constraint or alternative-rejected that drove this>') or a pointer to record an ADR — never a finished rationale you authored and never a rewrite of their change.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: At least one architecturally significant decision is quotable from the hand-written diff ([L<n>] cited) AND its WHY is recorded — a verbatim motivation sentence in the body, a reason-bearing commit subject, or a linked ADR/design-doc — quoted alongside the diff line, so a future maintainer can recover the intent without reverse-engineering the code.
- assessment=BAD: An architecturally significant decision (quoted from a hand-written hunk) ships with no recorded why anywhere in the body, the commit subjects, or a linked ADR — graded by severity: MINOR for a single contained signal (e.g. one new dependency or column), MAJOR for an auth/security, public-contract, or cross-cutting decision or two-plus unexplained signals. Stating the one constraint or alternative-rejected that drove the quoted change — in the body or an ADR — would preserve the decision for the next maintainer.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or diff.patch is absent/empty (no change to scan), OR no significance signal from the catalogue is quotable from a hand-written hunk (the change is routine — tests, formatting, docs, renames, or a version bump), so this decision-rationale practice has no surface to assess; the general adequacy of the PR description is deferred to describe-what-and-why and is not judged here.

## Data-model / DTO boundary (additive)
A plain serializable value type / DTO (a Swift Codable/Encodable/Decodable struct, a TS interface, a Java record/POJO) that is NOT bound to a persisted store, a migration, or a cross-process/wire contract is NOT a significant schema decision — emit NOT_APPLICABLE for it. An auth/security mechanism (token storage, login flow, key handling) IS significant even without a schema change.

## Client / UI significance ceiling (additive)
For client/app/UI code (a Swift/Kotlin/TS view, screen, rendering, effect, animation, or single app-internal class), a SINGLE new app-internal class or component is NOT automatically a cross-cutting pattern — adding one rendering/effect/view class that nothing else is built to extend is at most a single contained signal, so cap its missing-why at MINOR. Reserve MAJOR strictly for an auth/security mechanism, a wire/persistence/public-API contract change, a genuine cross-cutting mechanism the rest of the app plugs into (a base class many types extend, a shared interceptor/aspect/event bus), OR two or more distinct significance signals from the catalogue co-occurring unexplained. A lone undocumented app-internal class never reaches MAJOR on its own.
practices.whyItMatters: When you pick one design over another, the reasoning is the first thing to fade. A maintainer who cannot find why you chose it will either undo a good decision or be too afraid to touch it, and both cost the team later.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A meaningful design choice arrives with its reasoning written down, in the description or a short architecture decision record (ADR), such as 'chose an event queue over direct calls so a slow consumer cannot block checkout', not just the code that does it.

practices.name: Document public API and behaviour changes
practices.criteria:
GATE (apply FIRST): this practice concerns a PUBLIC/SHARED API surface — exported declarations a SEPARATE consumer compiles against (a library/framework target's public/open/exported symbols, a published package's API, a documented HTTP/RPC contract). A single application target with NO library product has no public API: internal type/field/private-signature changes there are NOT public-API changes -> emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Only fire when the diff changes an ACTUALLY-exported symbol. When the surface qualifies, assess whether the change to that PUBLIC/SHARED surface carries documentation for its consumers — a docstring/comment on the surface, a note in metadata.body, or a changelog entry in the diff — framed as a workflow habit about keeping consumers informed, never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. Read diff.patch (the FULL unified diff; each changed line carries an [L<n>] new-side marker), diff_summary.md (the file index), and metadata.body. Detect public-surface changes in the ADDED/MODIFIED lines, then check for accompanying documentation in the SAME diff or in metadata.body. Quote the exact [L<n>] span for every surface change you rely on and quote the documenting span (or state it is absent) — a span you cannot quote is counted absent.

## What counts as a public/shared surface (consumer-facing only)
A public surface is one another module, service, or caller depends on, identifiable from the added/removed lines: (a) an exported/public signature — a new or changed public function/method/class signature, a parameter added/removed/reordered, a return-type or visibility change on a symbol marked exported/public (export, public, pub, a capitalized Go identifier, __all__); (b) an HTTP route or its contract — a new/changed path, method, status code, request/response field; (c) a persisted or wire schema — a DB column/table, a protobuf/Avro/GraphQL/OpenAPI field, an event payload; (d) an error or response shape consumers branch on — a new/renamed error code, exception type, or response envelope field; (e) a config default or env-var/flag a consumer sets; (f) a declared breaking change. A purely internal/private symbol (no export marker, lower-case/package-private, a local var, a test helper, an inline refactor) is NOT a public surface — do not flag it. When in doubt about whether a symbol is exported, treat it as internal and prefer NOT_APPLICABLE over a false assessment=BAD.

## What counts as documentation (any one suffices)
Accompanying documentation is satisfied by ANY quotable span: (a) a docstring/doc-comment or inline comment ADDED in the diff ON or DIRECTLY ABOVE the changed surface that describes the new behaviour/parameter/field (a Javadoc/JSDoc/triple-quote//// block, an OpenAPI description:, a column/field comment); (b) a changelog/release-notes ADDITION in the diff (a file whose path contains CHANGELOG, HISTORY, RELEASES, or a docs/ entry, or a *.md under docs) that names this change; (c) a sentence in metadata.body that describes the consumer-facing effect of the surface change — quote it verbatim. A renamed-only doc, an unmodified template line, or prose that merely restates the title does NOT count. Generated/vendored files (OpenAPI specs emitted by codegen, *.gen.*, lockfiles, dist/, route trees) count as NEITHER a surface change to flag NOR as documentation — assess only the hand-written remainder per diff_summary.md.

## Non-overlap (enforce — never double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ONLY whether the public surface change is documented for consumers. It does NOT judge the PR's general what/why description (that is describe-what-and-why) nor whether a significant decision's rationale is recorded (that is records-significant-decisions-with-rationale): do not emit a finding here merely because metadata.body is thin or a design choice lacks justification — a finding requires a quotable PUBLIC-SURFACE change in the diff. If no public/shared surface changed, this practice does not apply regardless of description quality.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Determine, across the hand-written diff, the set S of quotable public-surface changes and, for each, whether it has quotable accompanying documentation. BREAKING (a removed/renamed public symbol, a removed/renamed route or response field, a narrowed type, a changed/removed config default, or a span the author labels breaking) with NO documentation anywhere -> MAJOR (consumers will break with no migration note). A non-breaking ADDITIVE public surface (new public function/route/field/error/flag) with NO documentation -> MINOR (consumers cannot discover or use it from the change alone). A public surface that IS documented, but the doc is thin (present yet does not mention the changed parameter/field/behaviour by name) -> INFO (the gentlest nudge to name the specific change). Every public surface change in S is documented -> PRESENT, GOOD. When S holds both documented and undocumented surfaces, grade by the MOST severe undocumented one. Identical diffs must always yield identical severity. Never exceed MAJOR.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every finding MUST quote at least one [L<n>] span proving a public-surface change, and either quote the documenting span or state which surfaces (named) have no doc on/above them, in metadata.body, and in any changelog/docs path in diff_summary.md. An assessment=BAD 'undocumented' claim is made only after naming all three checked locations (in-diff doc-comment, metadata.body, in-diff changelog/docs). Never assert a symbol is exported/public unless the export marker is visible in a quoted line. Never claim compile/runtime/correctness behaviour of the surface — assess only the documentation habit. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files, judge only the hand-written remainder; if no hand-written public surface remains, emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when at least one PUBLIC/SHARED surface change is quotable from the hand-written diff. A PRESENT, GOOD strength REQUIRES that quotable public surface — if every changed symbol is private/internal/non-exported, the practice has no surface and the finding is NOT_APPLICABLE, never a PRESENT, GOOD reading; there is no separate, lower documentation bar for private symbols (internal-comment hygiene is not assessed here). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE — do not fabricate a surface change to avoid looking idle, and do not flag an internal-only or docs/config-only diff.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: Every quotable public/shared surface change in the diff carries quotable documentation for consumers — a doc-comment on or above the changed signature/field, a changelog/docs addition naming it, or a sentence in metadata.body describing its consumer-facing effect (quote the surface [L<n>] span and the documenting span), so a consumer learns of the change from the change itself.
- assessment=BAD: At least one quotable public/shared surface change has no documentation on/above it, no naming sentence in metadata.body, and no changelog/docs addition (name all three locations checked) — graded by severity: INFO when documented but the doc omits the specific changed parameter/field, MINOR for an undocumented additive surface, MAJOR for an undocumented breaking change. Adding a one-line doc-comment on the surface or a changelog entry naming what consumers must do would let downstream callers adapt without reverse-engineering the diff.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: No public/shared surface change is quotable from the hand-written diff — the change is internal/private only, or is docs-only/config-only with no consumer-facing surface, or the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files — so the document-public-changes habit has no surface to apply to and no feedback is given.
practices.whyItMatters: People outside your code depend on what you export. Change a public function or endpoint without a note and they find out at runtime, in their own project, with no warning, and they trust your library a little less each time.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A renamed parameter or a changed response shape lands with its reference docs and a changelog line updated in the same change, so the people building on your API hear about it from you, not from a broken build.

areas.name: Disciplined delivery and version control
areas.description: Keep your delivery clean. Make each commit one self-contained change, leave generated and build files out of the diff, and branch from the shared integration branch so your work stands on its own.

practices.name: Keep each commit to one logical change
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the pull request's commits are SPLIT so that each one captures a single logical change rather than tangling unrelated work, framed as a workflow habit about how the history is partitioned — never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR on commit atomicity alone. Use ONLY metadata.commits[] (each entry has sha and message, where message is the commit SUBJECT line) together with the file-path table in diff_summary.md (the per-file changed-line spread).
