Per-commit patches are NOT in your context: you CANNOT see which files belong to which commit, so judge atomicity conservatively from the SUBJECTS and the overall file spread, and never claim a per-commit file mapping you cannot observe.

## What this judges (and what it explicitly does NOT)
This judges only the ATOMICITY/COHESION of the split — whether a single commit subject conjoins multiple unrelated concerns, or the series of subjects plainly mixes unrelated kinds of work (Pro Git 'commit early, commit often' as cohesive units; Conventional Commits 'one logical change per commit'; Herzig & Just on tangled changes degrading review and bisection). This practice DEFERS overall PR size and file spread to scope-one-reviewable-change (do NOT flag here merely because the change is large or touches many files), and DEFERS subject WORDING/legibility to commit-subjects-explain-each-change (do NOT flag here merely because a subject is vague, noisy, or non-imperative — a vague-but-cohesive subject is a PRESENT, GOOD strength here). Flag ONLY when the SPLIT itself is wrong: one commit doing several unrelated things, or a history that obviously braids unrelated concerns.

## Tangle signals (language-agnostic — quote the subject for each)
A commit subject CONJOINS unrelated concerns when it explicitly joins two or more distinct kinds of work with a conjunction or list, e.g. a fix AND a refactor ('fix null check and rename helpers'), a feature AND a format/lint sweep ('add export and reformat module'), a behavioural change AND a dependency bump, or 'and also'/'+'/';'/comma-separated clauses naming different concerns. A SERIES tangles when the subjects show clearly distinct concern-kinds interleaved with no separation — e.g. one subject is purely a wide format/lint/rename sweep AND another is a behavioural feature/fix, indicating unrelated work shipped together rather than as one cohesive unit. Two or more COHESIVE FEATURE commits coexisting in one PR is NOT a series tangle — multiple distinct features is a SIZE/scope matter deferred to scope-one-reviewable-change, not a wrong split; a series tangles ONLY when a process/chore concern-kind (format, lint, rename, merge-conflict resolution, or dependency bump) is interleaved with a behavioural feature/fix concern-kind. To claim a series-level tangle you MUST quote BOTH clashing subjects, and the chore subject you quote MUST itself name one of those enumerated chore kinds; if you cannot quote such a chore-vs-behaviour pair, there is NO series-level tangle (this does not change K, the per-commit conjoining count, which is judged separately), so absent any per-commit conjoining subject the observation is a PRESENT, GOOD strength. Do NOT count as a tangle: a single concern described in two clauses about the SAME change ('parse the header and validate it' is one logical step), a fix plus the test that covers it (cohesive), or a refactor that is itself the whole PR. When in doubt, treat it as cohesive — judge conservatively and never invent a tangle the subjects do not state.

## Closed chore-set gate (apply BEFORE any series-clash claim — the chore set is CLOSED and LITERAL)
A subject counts toward a SERIES-LEVEL clash ONLY if it literally contains one of: 'format', 'lint' / 'swiftlint', a rename-sweep (a wide 'rename X to Y across ...'), 'merge conflict' / 'merge develop' / 'merge main', or a dependency/version bump ('bump', 'upgrade <dep> to <version>', a version-number change). The verbs 'refactor', 'extract', 'wire', 'add', 'make', 'move', 'harden' are NOT chore-sweep kinds — they describe feature or design work, so a subject like 'Refactor AR services into ARConfigurationFactory' or 'Add OrbitGhostPathRenderer' is a normal feature/design commit and does NOT qualify as the chore half of a clash. If you cannot QUOTE a subject literally containing one of the enumerated chore tokens above, set series_clash = false → there is NO series-level tangle, so (absent any per-commit conjoining subject) the observation is a PRESENT, GOOD strength. Two cohesive feature/refactor commits coexisting is multiple-features (a scope matter), never a series clash.
## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
BEFORE counting per-commit conjoins, run the SERIES pre-scan: across all subjects, look for ANY pure process/chore subject (a wide format / lint / rename / merge-conflict-resolution / dependency-bump sweep) co-occurring with ANY behavioural feat/fix subject — if both are present that is a series-level clash worth at least MINOR; when multiple chore subjects are braided through the feature subjects, the history broadly tangles and that is MAJOR. The '+'/'and'/list signal counts as a per-commit conjoin ONLY when its clauses name DIFFERENT concern-kinds: 'add export AND reformat module' (feature + chore) conjoins; but 'add Vision + bounding box detection' or 'parse the header and validate it' name ONE concern in two clauses and do NOT conjoin — do not count them. Let T = the number of entries in metadata.commits[] and K = the number of commits whose own subject conjoins unrelated concerns by the rule above. State T and K, quote every conjoining subject you counted, and when you claim a SERIES-level tangle quote the two subjects whose concern-kinds clash.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
K == 0 AND no series-level tangle -> PRESENT, GOOD (commits read as cohesive logical units). K == 1 (one commit conjoins concerns) OR a single clear series-level concern-kind clash -> MINOR (one commit bundles unrelated work; splitting it would isolate each concern). K >= 2, OR K >= 1 together with a series-level clash, OR a series where most subjects each braid multiple concerns -> MAJOR (the history broadly tangles unrelated work, so a reviewer cannot reason about or revert one concern at a time). Never exceed MAJOR. Identical input (same subjects, same diff_summary.md table) must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Read the commit subjects from metadata.commits[] WHEN PRESENT; when that precompute field is absent, read the same subjects from any commit-subject list materialized in your context. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE only when NO commit subjects are observable from ANY source — the commit log could not be materialized; never treat missing commits as cohesive, and never NA on the mere absence of the metadata.commits[] field when the subjects are plainly listed in the artifact text. If T == 1, emit NOT_APPLICABLE: a single commit has no SPLIT to assess (a lone commit that internally does several things is a scope/subject matter, not an atomicity-of-split matter, and is assessed by other checks). metadata.commits[] is UNORDERED and DATE-LESS: never reason about commit ORDER, sequencing, or which change came first, and never claim a narrative the ordering would imply — judge the set of subjects, not a sequence. If the only non-merge entries reduce to one, or every entry is an automatic merge commit (a subject beginning 'Merge branch' or 'Merge remote-tracking'), emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Every finding MUST state T and K and quote the deciding subject(s).

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when metadata.commits[] holds two or more non-merge commits (enriched=true). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Do NOT attach a file or diff-line location to this finding — its evidence is the commit SUBJECT text you quote, not a diff line; attaching a stray file path can cause the finding to be dropped as out-of-diff-scope.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: Across the T commits (T stated, T >= 2), no subject conjoins unrelated concerns and the series shows no obvious clash of concern-kinds (K = 0), so each commit reads as one cohesive logical change a reviewer can reason about or revert in isolation.
- assessment=BAD: At least one commit subject conjoins unrelated work, or the series plainly braids distinct concern-kinds (K and T stated, each deciding subject quoted) — graded by severity: MINOR when exactly one commit bundles unrelated concerns (or one series-level clash), MAJOR when several commits do or the history broadly tangles. Splitting the bundled commit so each unrelated concern lands on its own would let a reviewer follow and revert one change at a time.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false, or metadata.commits[] is absent/empty, or only a single non-merge commit is present, or every entry is an automatic merge commit, so the atomicity of the commit split cannot be assessed.
practices.whyItMatters: A commit that mixes an unrelated refactor in with a bug fix cannot be cleanly reverted or cherry-picked, so you are stuck taking the bad with the good. One logical change per commit keeps your history a tool you can act on instead of a tangle.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Separate commits for separate ideas, such as 'Fix off-by-one in pagination' in one and 'Rename helper for clarity' in another, so each can be reviewed, reverted, or backported on its own.

practices.name: Keep generated and build files out of the diff
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this change keeps generated, built, and machine-emitted artifacts OUT of the diff, framed as a repository-hygiene habit about what gets committed — never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR. Judge ONLY from the file PATHS in the diff (the per-file table in diff_summary.md and the per-file headers in diff.patch). You MAY open diff.patch to confirm a flagged path is machine-emitted (e.g. a header banner like 'DO NOT EDIT', 'auto-generated', 'AUTOGENERATED', '@generated', or a minified single-line bundle) when the path alone is ambiguous, but the evidence is the PATH plus that confirming line — not a line-by-line code review of the file's contents.

## Non-overlap (enforce — do NOT double-flag a sibling)
This practice judges ONLY whether generated/build OUTPUT and committed cruft leaked into the change. It does NOT judge whether a dependency addition/removal was deliberate or justified — that is the sibling changes-dependencies-deliberately. A *-lock file (package-lock.json, pnpm-lock.yaml, yarn.lock, go.sum, Cargo.lock, poetry.lock, etc.) that moves IN STEP with a corresponding manifest edit (package.json, go.mod, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, Gemfile) in the SAME diff is a normal, regeneration-consistent lockfile bump — treat it as intended source movement and do NOT flag it here; the deliberateness question belongs to the sibling. Flag a lockfile under this practice ONLY when it is HAND-EDITED out of step with its manifest (lockfile changed with no matching manifest change in the diff, or hunks that hand-mutate resolved versions/integrity hashes a regenerate would own). A repository may DELIBERATELY version-control certain GENERATED sources — a committed OpenAPI spec, a generated API client, an ERD, or another regenerate-in-place artifact the project tracks on purpose and regenerates as part of its workflow (often carrying a regenerate command in its header or a CONTRIBUTING note). When such a tracked generated file moves IN STEP with the hand-written change that drives its regeneration, that is intended, regeneration-consistent movement — do NOT flag it; only flag generated/built OUTPUT that is NOT meant to be tracked (build dirs, bundles, coverage, caches, IDE/OS cruft) or a tracked generated file left STALE relative to its source.

## What counts as a generated/build/cruft path
Classify a changed path as ARTIFACT when its path matches a build-output or machine-emitted location: a build/output directory segment (dist/, build/, out/, target/, bin/, obj/.next/.nuxt/.svelte-kit/.output/, coverage/.nyc_output/, htmlcov/, __pycache__/, *.pyc, *.class, *.o, *.so under build output, site-packages/, vendor/ for committed vendored deps, node_modules/); editor/OS/tooling cruft (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db, *.swp, *.log under the tree.idea/.vscode/ workspace state); committed code-generation output that the repo regenerates (paths the handbook regenerates — *.gen.* such as routeTree.gen.ts, openapi.yaml / generated OpenAPI clients under api/**.gen.*, *.pb.go / *_pb2.py protobuf output, schema.mmd ERD output, query.gen.ts) carrying a 'DO NOT EDIT'/'@generated'/'auto-generated' banner; or a HAND-EDITED lockfile per the non-overlap rule above. A path is NOT an artifact merely for sitting near generated code — require the build/output directory segment, the cruft filename, or a quoted generated-banner line. Source files that legitimately live beside generated ones (hand-written code in src/, tests, docs, config you author) are NOT artifacts.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Let A = the count of changed ARTIFACT paths and F = metadata.changed_files (or the diff_summary.md row count when changed_files is absent). One artifact path -> MINOR (a stray build/cruft file slipped in — gitignore it and drop it from the change). Two to four artifact paths, OR any committed build-output DIRECTORY (dist/, build/, target/, out/, node_modules/, coverage/, __pycache__/) regardless of count, OR any hand-edited generated file that should be regenerated -> MAJOR (machine-emitted output is being reviewed as if it were source; regenerate and gitignore instead). Five or more artifact paths is still MAJOR — never escalate above MAJOR on this practice. Identical path sets must always yield identical severity. (The 1 vs 2-4 split and the directory-escalation are catalogue-chosen operational thresholds for consistency.)

## Generated-dominance abstention
When ARTIFACT paths account for essentially the WHOLE diff — A is at least 90% of F — the change is a regeneration/build commit, not a hand-authored change with leaked output, and there is no source remainder whose hygiene this practice can meaningfully judge: emit NOT_APPLICABLE rather than a blanket assessment=BAD on an intentional regeneration. Below that ratio, a hand-written remainder exists and the leaked artifacts are flaggable.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every assessment=BAD finding MUST quote each offending path verbatim from diff.patch / diff_summary.md, state A and F, and — for a generated-file (not directory, not cruft-filename) flag — quote the confirming banner line (e.g. '// Code generated by... DO NOT EDIT.') or note the path matches a handbook-regenerated artifact by name. A 'hand-edited lockfile' flag MUST state that no matching manifest path changed in the same diff (name the manifest you looked for). If you cannot quote an offending path, there is no artifact to flag. A PRESENT, GOOD strength requires that NO changed path classifies as ARTIFACT after the rules above. Never assert compile/runtime/build behavior from a path.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when the diff carries quotable file paths. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: Every changed path is a source/intended file (hand-written code, tests, docs, config you author, or a lockfile moving in step with its manifest); no build-output directory, no committed cruft, and no hand-edited generated file appears (A = 0 of F, with F stated), so a reviewer reads only what was authored.
- assessment=BAD: At least one changed path is machine-emitted output or committed cruft (A >= 1 of F, each path quoted) — graded by severity: MINOR for a single stray artifact file, MAJOR for two-to-four such files, any committed build-output directory, or any hand-edited generated file. The single most consequential next step is to add the path to.gitignore and drop it from the change (or, for a hand-edited generated file, regenerate it with its source command instead of editing the output by hand) so review sees source rather than build output.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The diff carries no quotable file paths, or ARTIFACT paths account for at least 90% of the changed files (a regeneration/build commit with no hand-authored remainder), so committed-artifact hygiene cannot be meaningfully assessed.
practices.whyItMatters: Committing a build output or an editor config bloats the diff with machine noise nobody reviews, and it creates merge conflicts in files no human wrote. Leaving them out gives the reviewer a change they can actually read.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: The diff holds only the source you hand-wrote, with build outputs, logs, and editor files kept out through .gitignore, and no generated bundle riding along unnoticed.

practices.name: Branch from the shared integration branch
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any 'assessment=GOOD' rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect — a branch cut off an in-flight feature branch rather than the shared integration branch. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when a branch was cut cleanly from the integration branch. A GOOD endorsement here would assert 'this branch was correctly cut from the integration line', which is an unverifiable clean bill of health, not an observed good act. Only two outcomes are available: presence=PRESENT, assessment=BAD (a quotable off-integration-branch signal) or NOT_APPLICABLE (no quotable off-integration-branch signal, or no surface). Assess whether this change appears to have been branched off the team's integration branch rather than stacked on top of an in-flight feature branch — framed as a version-control delivery habit about where the work was cut from, never as a code-defect claim and never as an assertion that the history is broken. Judge from the mounted repository at inputs/sources/scm/repo (a full clone — inspect its git history) and metadata.json (which carries source_branch, target_branch, and the commit list): determine whether the branch was cut cleanly from the integration branch (main, master, dev, or develop). Count how many commits this branch carries that the integration branch does not, and how many distinct authors appear across them; a long-lived branch many commits ahead with several distinct authors suggests it was NOT freshly branched off the integration branch. Surface the facts and judge — there is no precomputed boolean to defer to. You do NOT see the diff or the commit subjects here; reason only about the branch facts. NEVER claim a merge will conflict, that the history is wrong, or that any commit is bad — you are pointing at a branching habit, not auditing the tree.

## What the signal means (and its limits)
When a branch is cut from main/develop and worked solo, its ahead-range is small and authored by one person. A large ahead-range carrying commits from SEVERAL distinct authors is the tell that the branch was started from another in-flight feature branch (inheriting that branch's unmerged commits) rather than from the shared integration line — which makes the PR hard to review in isolation and couples its merge to the other branch landing first. Many commits ahead of the integration branch AND more than one distinct author across them is a HEURISTIC suggesting a from-feature-branch cut, not proof — a long-lived solo branch that legitimately merged main back in can also accrue multiple authors. Treat it as a habit nudge, never as a certainty.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MINOR (the only non-positive band): the ONLY firing signal is the CONCRETE one: targetBranch is not a conventional integration branch (main/master/dev/develop). Do NOT fire on commit-count, prCommitCount, or an inferred author-count — a long-lived solo branch that merged main back in accrues commits and authors legitimately, so those are speculative and stay SILENT. Name targetBranch as the quoted evidence; a single, calibrated nudge to branch from the integration branch next time and, if intended, to say so in the description. Never escalate above MINOR: this is a low-cost habit hint computed from a heuristic, not a blocker. Identical branch facts must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If the source/target branch names (metadata.json) and the mounted git history are unavailable or do not show a clear off-integration-branch cut, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — there is no branch-origin signal to act on; judge only from the actual branch names and commit history, and never assert the off-feature-branch habit on a weak or ambiguous signal. The precompute supplies only prCommitCount (the PR's commit-list length) and targetIsIntegration — NOT the true ahead-range or its authors, which are not in metadata. To judge a long-lived off-integration branch, inspect the git history at inputs/sources/scm/repo (the ahead-range vs the integration branch and the distinct authors across it). Every finding MUST name the targetBranch and quote whatever history facts you actually read. If neither metadata.json nor the mounted git history carries any branch-origin signal — no source/target branch names and no inspectable ahead-range — you may NOT assert the branch was cut off a non-integration branch; you have no evidence for that habit, so abstain rather than infer it from silence.

## Observation & abstention
Emit an assessment=BAD finding only when the concrete firing signal from the ## Severity gate is true — targetBranch is not a conventional integration branch (main/master/dev/develop) — quoting targetBranch as the sole evidence; otherwise NOT_APPLICABLE. Do NOT fire on a commits-ahead count, an author count, or any inferred ahead-range pattern — those stay SILENT per the ## Severity gate. There is no PRESENT, GOOD strength for this habit — a branch correctly cut from the integration line simply produces no signal (the off-integration-branch signal false) and the practice abstains rather than praising the absence of a problem.
- assessment=BAD: the branch names (metadata.json) show targetBranch is not a conventional integration branch (main/master/dev/develop) — quote targetBranch verbatim as the sole evidence — the pattern of a branch cut from an in-flight feature branch rather than the integration branch. Cutting the next branch from the up-to-date integration branch keeps the change reviewable and mergeable on its own.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: the branch names (metadata.json) + the mounted git history is absent, or the off-integration-branch signal is false or missing, so there is no off-integration-branch signal to assess (a branch cut cleanly from the integration line produces no signal here).
practices.whyItMatters: Cutting your branch off a half-finished feature branch drags all of its unmerged work into yours, tangling the two and making your diff impossible to review on its own. Branching from the shared integration branch, the one everyone merges into, often main, keeps your work independent.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A branch cut fresh from the integration branch that carries only your own commits, so the diff shows your change alone and not a pile of someone else's in-flight work.

areas.name: Tracking and planning the work
areas.description: Plan and track the work so a maintainer can route it and watch it progress. Split genuinely large issues into trackable sub-tasks, and triage each one with the labels, assignee, or milestone that tells the team where it belongs.

practices.name: Break large work into trackable subtasks
practices.criteria:
Judge ONLY whether a LEGITIMATELY large or multi-part issue is broken into explicit, trackable sub-units — task-list checkboxes, sub-issues, or referenced child issues — rather than a monolithic ask carrying multiple distinct parts with no breakdown. Read ONLY the title, body, labels[], the sub_issues_total/sub_issues_completed rollup in metadata.json, and comments[].body. There is NO diff and no code in your context. Never assert technical feasibility, hour-estimates, or design correctness; never praise or grade the reporter. This practice asks whether large work is DECOMPOSED, not whether it should have been one concern — when an issue genuinely bundles unrelated deliverables, that is assessed by a different check (INTERNAL ROUTING — do not write this relationship into reasoning or guidance); here a multi-part ask is treated as legitimately large work that needs a breakdown, never as a scoping defect to re-flag.

## What a breakdown is (each requires a quotable span)
Count the issue as DECOMPOSED when ANY of these is quotable: (a) a Markdown TASK list — two or more lines beginning '- [ ]' or '- [x]' that each name a UNIT OF WORK TO DO (a sub-step to implement) — in the body or a comment. Note the distinction: an ACCEPTANCE-CRITERIA / Definition-of-Done checklist states CONDITIONS to VERIFY ('- [ ] the API returns 404 for an unknown id'), not work to partition; an AC/DoD list is owned by issue-has-checkable-outcome and does NOT by itself count as a work-breakdown here, though a list that mixes verifiable conditions with concrete implementation sub-steps does count; (b) sub_issues_total >= 2 in the rollup (a tracked sub-issue tree), quoting the rollup figures; (c) two or more explicit references to child/tracking issues that enumerate the parts (e.g. 'Part 1: #41', 'Tracked in #41, #42, #43', a quoted list of '#N' items that the body frames as sub-parts). A single '#N' mention with no enumeration, a bulleted prose list with no checkboxes, or a numbered narrative paragraph is NOT a trackable breakdown — quote what is present and count it absent. A lone checkbox ('- [ ]' appearing once) is not a list; require two or more checkbox lines.

## What makes work LARGE (the gate for requiring a breakdown)
Treat the issue as LARGE/multi-part ONLY when at least one is quotable: (i) an epic/initiative/meta label whose name case-insensitively contains 'epic', 'initiative', 'meta', or 'tracking'; (ii) the body enumerates >=2 SEPARABLE deliverables in prose that could ship or be assigned INDEPENDENTLY (distinct features/endpoints/screens, an 'and also…' chain of unrelated capabilities, or 'Phase 1 … Phase 2 …' where each phase is independently shippable) — the gate fires at two-or-more so it agrees with the severity bands below (exactly 2 enumerated parts -> INFO, >=3 -> MINOR); sequential sub-steps of ONE cohesive end-to-end flow (e.g. 'register, then log in, then sync, then access the workspace' for a single secure-access feature) are ONE deliverable described as a journey, NOT three parts, so do NOT count user-journey steps of a single capability as distinct deliverables, and when in doubt whether the spans are one journey or several deliverables prefer NOT_APPLICABLE; (iii) sub_issues_total >= 2 already present (the author has signalled a multi-part tree). Quote the spans that establish largeness. A focused single-concern issue (one deliverable, one bug, one capability) is NOT large — it neither needs nor is penalised for the absence of a checklist.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = an epic/initiative/meta/tracking-labelled issue (signal i) with NO quotable breakdown of any kind — a tracking artifact with nothing to track. MINOR = a non-epic body that enumerates >=3 distinct deliverable parts in prose (signal ii) with no quotable task list, sub-issue rollup, or child-issue enumeration. INFO = exactly 2 distinct enumerated parts in prose with no breakdown (the lightest nudge — barely past single-step). PRESENT, GOOD (no severity) = a quotable breakdown is present, OR the issue is not large by the gate above. Never exceed MAJOR. Identical input must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
For every element you mark present — a checkbox list, the rollup figures, a child-issue enumeration, an epic label, or an enumerated part — quote the exact span from the body, a label name, a comment, or the rollup; an element you cannot quote is counted absent. A 'no breakdown' assessment=BAD finding requires that you have scanned and named all three surfaces (body, the sub_issues rollup, comments) and found none quotable. A 'large' claim requires a quoted epic-label name, the >=3 quoted part-spans, or the rollup figure — never infer largeness from tone or title length. If the body is empty, title-only, or an unmodified template, there is no quotable content to establish largeness OR a breakdown -> NOT_APPLICABLE.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when largeness and the breakdown state are both quotable-decidable. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the issue is a pure question / support request / open discussion with no concrete deliverable, when the body is empty/title-only/template (nothing to assess), or when it is a small single-concern deliverable (the breakdown question does not arise — never mark such an issue assessment=BAD for lacking a checklist). Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior.

Guidance is feed-forward (Hattie & Timperley 'Where to next?'): when a large issue has no breakdown, name that the work spans the parts you quoted and show ONLY the artifact to add — a '## Tasks' heading followed by a '- [ ] <first sub-step>' checklist skeleton, or a 'Tracked in: #…' line for child issues — never author the actual sub-tasks for the reporter, never enumerate every part.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: Either a trackable breakdown is quotable (a task list of two or more '- [ ]' items, a sub_issues_total >= 2 rollup, or an enumerated set of child-issue references — quote it), OR the issue is a focused single-concern deliverable that the largeness gate does not trigger, so no breakdown is needed. The large work is decomposed into units a maintainer can track to done, satisfying epic→story decomposition and INVEST Small.
- assessment=BAD: A clearly large or multi-part ask carries no trackable breakdown — graded by severity: MAJOR when an epic/initiative/meta/tracking-labelled issue (quote the label) has no checklist, sub-issue rollup, or child-issue enumeration; MINOR when the body enumerates three or more distinct deliverable parts in prose (quote them) with no breakdown; INFO when exactly two parts are enumerated with none. Adding a '## Tasks' checklist or linking the parts as child issues would let each piece be picked up and tracked to done on its own.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue is a pure question, support request, or open discussion with no concrete deliverable; or the body is empty, title-only, or an unmodified template so neither largeness nor a breakdown is quotable; or it is a small single-concern deliverable that the largeness gate does not trigger, so the breakdown question does not arise and no feedback is given.
practices.whyItMatters: A big ask written as one wall of text is either 0% or 100% done, with no way to see progress or split the load. Breaking it into checkable steps turns a daunting job into pieces people can pick up and finish one at a time.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A genuinely large issue split into a task list or linked sub-issues, such as 'design the schema', 'add the endpoint', and 'wire up the UI', so progress is visible and pieces can be worked in parallel.

practices.name: Triage the issue with labels and ownership
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this issue carries categorizing metadata that lets a maintainer ROUTE and PLAN it — read ONLY the metadata.labels[] array, metadata.assignees (or metadata.assignee), and metadata.milestone, plus comments[].body for a closing question-classification check. This is a triage-metadata habit about whether the issue is routable at a glance; it is NOT a judgment of the issue's PROSE (problem statement, scope, definition of done) — that belongs to the sibling G3 practices issue-states-an-actionable-problem / issue-scoped-to-single-concern / issue-has-checkable-outcome and is deferred to them. Never assert that an applied label is the CORRECT one, never claim an assignee is the right owner, and never run code or judge whether the work is worth doing. You assess only PRESENCE of routing metadata, not its accuracy.

## Source of signals (single authoritative source)
Use ONLY: (1) metadata.labels[] — each entry's name; (2) metadata.assignees[] / metadata.assignee — whether at least one human owner is set; (3) metadata.milestone — whether a milestone/iteration is set. Do NOT infer a label from body prose, and do NOT treat a template heading, a section in the body, or an issue-form field as a label. A label exists only if it appears in labels[]. State the exact label names, assignee presence, and milestone presence you used.

## What counts as a categorizing label (vocabulary-agnostic)
Be GENERALIZATION-careful: do NOT require any specific label namespace, prefix, or color, and do NOT require a maintainer's house taxonomy (no 'type::', 'area/', 'priority:' is mandated). A categorizing label is ANY label whose name plausibly assigns a TYPE (e.g. names containing 'bug', 'defect', 'feature', 'enhancement', 'story', 'task', 'chore', 'epic'), an AREA/COMPONENT (e.g. 'backend', 'frontend', 'ui', 'api', 'docs', 'infra', a module or team name), or a PRIORITY/SEVERITY/EFFORT signal (e.g. 'priority', 'p0'.'p3', 'critical', 'blocker', 'severity', 'size', 'effort'). Treat a routing-affecting status/triage label (e.g. 'triage', 'needs-triage', 'ready', 'blocked') as categorizing too. EXCLUDE purely social/process-noise labels that do NOT help route or plan: 'good first issue', 'help wanted', 'duplicate', 'wontfix', 'invalid', 'question', 'discussion', 'stale', 'spam'. If labels[] contains ONLY excluded labels, treat it as carrying no categorizing label.

## Triviality / abstention gate (mandatory)
Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the issue is a pure QUESTION or open DISCUSSION rather than a unit of work to be routed: a 'question'/'discussion'/'support' label is present, OR the body is interrogative-only with no asserted defect and no requested capability. A discussion thread has nothing to triage onto a board, so never demand a type/area/priority label of it. ALSO emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the metadata needed to judge presence is itself unavailable: metadata.enriched is false, OR labels[], assignees, AND milestone are all absent fields (not merely empty) so presence cannot be determined — never read missing metadata as an empty/untriaged set. An empty body with NO categorizing label, assignee, or milestone on a substantive issue is NOT excused here — that is the assessment=BAD case, not abstention.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion; never exceeds MINOR)
This is a lightweight routing-hygiene nudge, never a blocker. Apply the bands in strict precedence — a categorizing label takes priority over an assignee/milestone, which takes priority over nothing — so each metadata state maps to exactly ONE band. PRESENT, GOOD (no severity) when a categorizing label is present (the issue is typed, area'd, or prioritized for board filtering), regardless of whether an assignee or milestone is also set. INFO when NO categorizing label is present but at least one weak routing signal is — an assignee OR a milestone is set (the issue is owned/scheduled but still uncategorized for board filtering); a gentle nudge to add a type/area label. MINOR when the issue has NO categorizing label AND NO assignee AND NO milestone at all (it is unroutable — a maintainer cannot filter, assign, or schedule it from metadata). Never escalate above MINOR on triage metadata alone. Identical metadata must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
Every finding MUST state the concrete signals it used: the categorizing label name(s) found (quote the exact name), whether an assignee is set, and whether a milestone is set. A PRESENT, GOOD strength REQUIRES naming the categorizing label that satisfies it (e.g. the categorizing label 'backend'). A MINOR REQUIRES confirming all three are absent — quote that labels[] is empty or contains only excluded labels (name them), assignees is empty, and milestone is null. Do NOT claim a label is present without quoting its name.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD (INFO/MINOR) finding only when the metadata is available (enriched=true and at least one of labels[]/assignees/milestone is a present field) and the issue is a unit of work. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward (Hattie & Timperley 'Where to next?'): name the single most useful missing routing signal and show the SHAPE to add (e.g. 'add a type or area label so this can be filtered on the board', or 'set a priority label so it can be ranked'), never invent which specific label is correct, never assert an owner. Do not double-flag prose quality — if the issue's problem statement is weak but a categorizing label is present, this practice is still a PRESENT, GOOD strength and defers the prose to the G3 siblings.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: A categorizing label is present — type, area/component, or priority/severity/effort; quote its exact name — so a maintainer can filter and route the issue from its metadata without a triage round-trip. An assignee or milestone WITHOUT a categorizing label does NOT qualify here; that is the INFO case below. Whether the chosen label is the RIGHT one is not judged; presence is sufficient.
- assessment=BAD: Grade by severity. INFO when an assignee OR milestone is set but labels[] carries no categorizing label (owned/scheduled yet uncategorized for board filtering) — a light nudge to add a type/area label. MINOR when the issue has NO categorizing label, NO assignee, and NO milestone at all (confirm each absent, naming any excluded-only labels), leaving it unroutable: the feedback names the single most useful signal to add (a type/area label, a priority, or an owner) so the next issue lands on a board ready to plan.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue is a pure question, support request, or open discussion with no unit of work to route, so triage metadata does not apply and no feedback is given; OR metadata.enriched is false / labels[], assignees, and milestone are all absent fields so presence cannot be determined. This abstention prevents demanding routing labels of a discussion thread and prevents reading missing metadata as an untriaged set.
practices.whyItMatters: An untriaged issue is invisible at planning: no one knows whose area it is, how urgent it is, or which release it belongs to, so it sinks to the bottom of the backlog. A few labels make it routable at a glance.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: An issue tagged with its type and area, assigned to an owner, and placed in a milestone, so a maintainer scanning the board can route and plan it without opening it.

practices.name: Confirm the outcome before closing the issue
practices.criteria:
Assess, at the moment an issue was CLOSED, whether its own stated definition-of-done was actually met — every checkable acceptance item ticked and every tracked sub-issue completed — rather than the issue being closed with a checklist item still unchecked or sub-issues still open. Framed strictly as a lifecycle habit about confirming the outcome before closing, NEVER as a claim that the work is wrong or incomplete in fact: the unchecked box may simply never have been ticked while the work was genuinely done. Read ONLY metadata.json (the issue title, body, labels[], state, state_reason, the sub_issues_total / sub_issues_completed rollup, and closed_at) plus comments[].body. There is NO diff and no code in your context — you read only the issue text, its labels, and its rollup.

## Scope gate (this practice fires only on a real close)
This is the retrospective sibling of issue-has-checkable-outcome: that practice owns whether a NEW issue DEFINES a checkable outcome; THIS one owns the single at-close question of whether that outcome was confirmed before the issue was closed. To stay in scope you MUST observe state == 'CLOSED'. If state is not 'CLOSED', the issue is not closed, so there is no close to assess -> NOT_APPLICABLE. The DISPOSITION of a close is read from the observable fact, in priority order: use metadata.state_reason WHEN PRESENT (e.g. 'COMPLETED', 'completed', 'NOT_PLANNED', 'not_planned' — quote the exact value); OTHERWISE read the disposition from a closing comment or the body — an explicit not-planned / wontfix / won't-fix / duplicate / dupe / superseded / abandoned / deferred signal marks it as NOT-DONE. When NEITHER a state_reason NOR an explicit not-planned/duplicate/wontfix textual signal is present, a bare state == 'CLOSED' DEFAULTS to a completed/done close (do NOT invent or report a state_reason value the artifact never carried — say only that the issue is closed with no explicit not-planned signal). NOT-PLANNED CARVE-OUT: when the disposition (from state_reason OR a closing-comment/body signal) indicates the issue was closed as NOT PLANNED / WONTFIX / DUPLICATE / superseded / deferred (the team decided NOT to do the work), an unmet checklist is the EXPECTED, correct state — the outcome was deliberately abandoned, not silently skipped — so emit NOT_APPLICABLE and stay silent; this practice fires only when the close signals the work was meant to be DONE (a completed/default close).

## What a checkable outcome is (each requires a quotable span)
The issue carried a checkable outcome when ANY of these is quotable from the body or a comment: (a) a task-list — one or more lines of '- [ ]' / '- [x]' (GitHub/GitLab checkbox syntax); (b) an acceptance-criteria / Definition-of-Done heading followed by checkable items; (c) a Given-When-Then block. The sub-issue rollup is a checkable outcome on its own when sub_issues_total > 0 (quote sub_issues_completed of sub_issues_total). If NONE of these is quotable, the issue defined no checkable outcome to confirm against -> NOT_APPLICABLE (never demand acceptance criteria a closed issue never stated, and never fabricate a checklist).

## ORDERED DISPOSITION SCAN (mandatory — run AFTER signal-1 fires, BEFORE finalizing MAJOR)
The instant an unmet-outcome signal fires, you MUST, in order: (1) scan EVERY closing comment and the body for a disposition span — a clause naming where/how the work was handled or why it stops: 'handled by' / 'handled with' / 'implemented in' / 'implemented with' / 'covered by' / 'done in' / 'addressed in' / 'superseded by' / 'tracked elsewhere' / 'tracked in' / 'deferred' / 'follow-up' (and quotable issue/MR references like 'US 3.3', '#123', '!45'); (2) if such a span is found, the disposition WAS named at close → CAP at MINOR and QUOTE that span in reasoning; (3) only if NO disposition span exists anywhere may you finalize MAJOR — and a MAJOR's reasoning MUST explicitly assert that every closing comment and the body were scanned and none named a disposition. A bare unchecked box with no naming comment drives MAJOR; an unchecked box accompanied by a closing comment that names the disposition is at most MINOR. Never finalize MAJOR without performing this scan.

## The two unmet-outcome signals (quote the exact span)
With state == 'CLOSED' on a completed/default close, the outcome was left unconfirmed when EITHER signal holds:
(1) the body or a comment carries a task-list / acceptance-criteria block with at least one UNCHECKED item — a line beginning '- [ ]' (an empty checkbox) under a done-defining heading; quote that exact unchecked line.
(2) sub_issues_total > 0 AND sub_issues_completed < sub_issues_total — tracked child work was still open at close; quote 'sub_issues_completed of sub_issues_total' (e.g. '2 of 5').
State which signal fired and quote its span. A checklist whose every item is '- [x]' AND (sub_issues_total == 0 OR sub_issues_completed == sub_issues_total) means the outcome WAS confirmed -> PRESENT, GOOD.

## What this practice does NOT assert (safety rail)
Never claim the work is actually incomplete, broken, or that closing was wrong — an unchecked box is an UNCONFIRMED outcome, not a proven gap; the author may have completed the work and simply not ticked the box, or tracked the remainder elsewhere. Describe only the observable state ('this issue was closed as completed with the item "<quoted unchecked line>" still unchecked' / 'with sub-issues 2 of 5 completed'), never a charge against the author ('you closed it unfinished' / 'you left the work broken'). The feed-forward lesson is the habit: before closing, confirm each criterion is met — tick the boxes, or close the remaining child issues, or leave a comment noting which items were deferred and filing a follow-up for them. Talk about the issue and the next step, never grade the closer.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion; never exceeds MAJOR)
MAJOR = closed as completed/default AND at least one UNCHECKED acceptance-criteria / Definition-of-Done item remains (signal 1) — the issue's own stated done-condition was not all ticked at close. MINOR = closed as completed/default AND signal 1 does NOT hold but sub_issues_completed < sub_issues_total (signal 2 only) — tracked child work was still open, a lighter lapse since the parent's own body had no unchecked done-item. (When BOTH fire, take MAJOR.) CLOSING-COMMENT DE-ESCALATION (apply before finalizing): when a closing comment (or the body) explicitly names the disposition of the unchecked items — saying which were deferred, tracked elsewhere, or done-but-not-ticked, or filing/linking a follow-up for the remainder — the outcome WAS addressed at close even if a box stayed empty, so CAP the severity at MINOR (the confirmation habit was largely honoured). The unchecked box ALONE, with no naming comment, is what drives MAJOR. PRESENT, GOOD (no severity) = closed with every checkable item ticked and every tracked sub-issue completed. Never exceed MAJOR on outcome-confirmation alone. Identical inputs must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
For every element you mark present — an unchecked '- [ ]' line, the rollup figures, the state, the state_reason — quote the exact span (or the exact metadata value). An assessment=BAD finding MUST quote the deciding span: the unchecked checklist line, or 'sub_issues_completed of sub_issues_total'. A 'no checkable outcome' or 'not a completed close' abstention is reached only after naming what you scanned (body, comments, rollup, state_reason). If state is not 'CLOSED', or state_reason indicates NOT PLANNED / WONTFIX / DUPLICATE, or no checkable outcome is quotable, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior, and never read a missing rollup as zero.

## Observation & abstention
Emit a PRESENT, GOOD or assessment=BAD finding only when state == 'CLOSED' on a completed/default close AND a checkable outcome is quotable. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Guidance is feed-forward and addressed to the closing habit, not the author: point forward to ticking the remaining item, closing the open child issues, or filing a follow-up for the deferred part before closing next time — never name the presence or assessment, the severity, or a band, and never assert the work itself is wrong.

## Observations
- PRESENT, GOOD: The issue was closed as completed (quote state == 'CLOSED' and the completed/default state_reason) with its stated outcome confirmed — every '- [x]' ticked and (sub_issues_total == 0 OR sub_issues_completed == sub_issues_total), quoting the rollup — so the definition-of-done was met before the issue was closed.
- assessment=BAD: The issue was closed as completed (quote state and state_reason) but its stated outcome was left unconfirmed — graded by severity: MAJOR when an acceptance-criteria / Definition-of-Done item is still unchecked (quote the '- [ ]' line); MINOR when the body has no unchecked done-item but sub_issues_completed < sub_issues_total (quote 'N of M'). Ticking the remaining item, closing the open child issues, or leaving a comment that names which items were deferred and files a follow-up for them would confirm the outcome before closing. This never asserts the work is incomplete in fact — only that the outcome was not confirmed against the issue's own checklist.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: state is not 'CLOSED' (the issue is not closed, so the at-close confirmation question does not arise — defining a checkable outcome on an open issue belongs to the sibling issue-has-checkable-outcome); OR state_reason indicates the issue was closed as NOT PLANNED / WONTFIX / DUPLICATE (an unmet checklist is the expected state for deliberately-abandoned work); OR the body and comments carry no quotable checkable outcome and sub_issues_total is 0 or absent, so there is no stated done-condition to confirm the close against.
practices.whyItMatters: Closing an issue tells the whole team it is handled. Closing it with a checklist item still unticked or sub-issues still open drops that work quietly, and someone hits the gap later expecting it to be done.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Before closing, every acceptance item is checked and every tracked sub-issue is finished, or a comment says plainly what is being dropped and why, so 'closed' honestly means done.
